author: scottmcm

body:
It looks like there's a bug causing an assertion failure when building the standard library. I've given it a look and offered a guess at what's causing it below. There's still work to do here beyond fixing that, though.

First, could you add a ui test to demonstrate that this fixes https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/147875? It looks like it might not yet, since the code for scheduling unwind drops on calls panicking looks unchanged.

Second, after verifying that this results in the correct borrow-checking behavior, we need to make sure that this change doesn't negatively affect codegen. Per the old comment on `needs_cleanup`, at least at the time it was written, LLVM didn't handle the unnecessary cleanup blocks and `StorageDead`s particularly well. If you can demonstrate with codegen tests that that's not an issue anymore, and perf isn't too bad, that might be all that's needed. But my expectation is that we'll have to get rid of or ignore the `StorageDead`s later in compilation (sometime after they serve their purpose in borrowck). Unless there's a reason to keep the `StorageDead`s around longer, my gut feeling is that this cleanup would be best as a post-borrowck MIR pass (maybe as part of `CleanupPostBorrowck`?), since then optimization passes can be done on cleaner MIR and we can test it works with MIR tests rather than codegen tests. Could you also add a test for this not affecting later stages of compilation? If you accomplish that by removing the unwind-path `StorageDead`s as part of a MIR pass, that'd be a mir-opt test.

Before you push again, you'll probably want to run the codegen and mir-opt tests to make sure the former is clean and to bless the latter. Regardless of what approach we take here, if we're changing how the MIR is built, there should be differences in the MIR building test output (part of the mir-opt suite).

*[View changes since this review](https://triagebot.infra.rust-lang.org/gh-changes-since/rust-lang/rust/149435/cc3eee7fbe17ea4b7238531cb97e1b7b8bd6afce..5afe7c2781141f4b3949774ca14b30ce404ee738)*
author: dianne

body: If I had to guess, that ICE is because this `storage_dead_on_unwind` argument to `build_scope_drops` is being passed `is_coroutine && needs_cleanup`; it's `false` when the body isn't a coroutine, so `unwind_to` doesn't get updated. Probably this argument can be removed entirely, given that we're always adding `StorageDead`s to `unwind_drops` and we're always emitting `StorageDead`s when we need a cleanup block? The comments in this method will need updating as well.
author: dianne

body:
Could you please explain this change? My understanding at least is that the ordering of `StorageDead`s only matters relative to actual drops, since that's when it can affect borrow-checking; reordering `StorageDead`s amongst each other won't do anything, but a `StorageDead` before a drop terminator will cause a borrow-checking failure if the destructor could reference the dead memory. As such, I'd expect we'd only need a cleanup block when there's actual drops (which is what the old version of this was checking for). Is there an edge case where we'd need a cleanup block with only `StorageDead`s in it? Otherwise, could you reinstate the comment about avoiding creating landing pads when there's no actual destructors?

That said, from what I can tell this method is only used for determining whether cleanup blocks are required for unwinding from panics in destructors, so could you make sure there's a MIR test checking that we don't create unnecessary cleanup blocks in other cases too, particularly for calls?

Also, if the comment about LLVM is still true and we need to get rid of the `StorageDead`s before codegen, we should probably keep some updated version of those comments around.
author: dianne

body:
It's possible we'll hit these debug asserts too since `break_for_tail_call` also only seems to adjust `unwind_to` for actual drops. Could you add a test for explicit tail calls to make sure it doesn't break with `Storage` and `ForLint` drops getting added to `unwind_drops` (and fix it if it does break)?

I wonder if there's a less fragile way to do this... maybe that's in part what the `` // FIXME(explicit_tail_calls) code duplication with `build_scope_drops` `` is about. It could be worth considering as a first step if a lot of entrypoints into the unwind drop tree end up needing changes ^^
author: dianne

body: I believe this also needs changing. This is where `unwind_drops` gets populated with the drops needed for calls' panic paths, so I'd expect fixing https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/147875 will require adjusting this.
author: dianne

body: Yeah, so all comments now reflect that StorageDead is emitted on unwind paths for all functions (not just coroutines) for borrow-checking purposes.

This still needs CI to pass before I can review it properly. I've left a few comments on obvious things, but I don't think reviewing the code changes would be helpful at this point. Please test your changes locally. You don't have to run the whole test suite yourself, but for this change, you'll at least want make sure that the mir-opt and codegen tests all pass, that any relevant ui tests pass, and that tidy passes as well.

Could you rebase onto a more recent commit, also? I don't expect there will be conflicts in the MIR building part of this, but I'm not sure about the rest.

I don't mean to be harsh, but this is a relatively complex and nuanced change. If you're not familiar with what's being changed, why it's being changed, the consequences/needs of that, and general contribution procedure, I'd recommend gaining familiarity with easier issues instead.

*[View changes since this review](https://triagebot.infra.rust-lang.org/gh-changes-since/rust-lang/rust/149435/ef2657cbaf6885b71e5ffd277ffe72a2616c0a7c..59a7e5628180cf642e923c621c8ef673170d987a)*
author: dianne

body:
My previous comment about this stands: if the remarks about LLVM still hold and we need to get rid of the `StorageDead`s before codegen, some version of that comment should be preserved and we'll need to get rid of the `StorageDead`s before codegen. I'm not seeing anything to do with that in this PR. As I suggested previously, I think a MIR transform may be cleanest, possibly as part of the post-analysis cleanup pass.

"Proper drop ordering" is also a bit misleading, since this doesn't affect runtime drop order. We only want a change in static semantics out of this. I realize I also mentioned drop order being enforced; I was being lax with terminology (I haven't found a great way to express "we want to treat locals as dead for the purpose of checking whether they might be referenced by destructors, like how we do normally"). My bad as far as communication on the PR goes, but code comments should be precise since they'll be read by people who aren't following this discussion.

Since this is pretty subtle, I think this comment should explain why `StorageDead`s are only needed when value/linting drops are present, why we want them to be present in that case, and what we do with them later to make sure they don't affect codegen (or, if they already don't affect codegen, that should be mentioned here too).

More importantly, you'll need non-explicit-tail-call-related tests, and mir-building tests will need updating. I'm not seeing any test changes related to what this PR is doing.

body:
Thanks for the feedback. I've addressed these points.
I removed the explicit tail call test (tail-call-storage-dead-unwind.rs) and I added tests for the static semantics change:

I also added mir-opt tests to verify StorageDead presence and removal:
```
tests/mir-opt/building/storage_dead_unwind_path.rs — Shows StorageDead is present in built.after.mir on unwind paths
tests/mir-opt/storage_dead_removed_after_borrowck.rs — Shows StorageDead is removed by CleanupPostBorrowck (present in built.after.mir, removed in CleanupPostBorrowck.after.mir)
```

I updated the mir-building tests by blessing them to reflect the new StorageDead statements on unwind paths. The diffs show StorageDead appearing in cleanup blocks where it wasn't present before.
All the new tests are general and don't involve explicit tail calls—they test regular function calls that might panic, which is the core scenario this PR addresses.I also verify that StorageDead is emitted on unwind paths, removed after borrow-checking, and that the borrow-checker behavior is consistent. All tests pass, and the mir-opt tests show that StorageDead is properly removed by the MIR transform pass.

I'll update the comments to explain why StorageDeads are only needed when value/linting drops are present (they're only relevant for borrow-checking when there are destructors that might reference the dead variable).

Explain why we want them in that case (to ensure the borrow-checker treats locals as dead at the same point on all paths, for static semantics consistency).

Clarify what we do with them later (CleanupPostBorrowck removes them from cleanup blocks after borrow-checking, so they don't affect codegen; normal-path StorageDead is removed later by RemoveStorageMarkers).

I'll update the comments to reflect this and make the rationale clearer.

body: I don't think this is true. `RemoveStorageMarkers` removes storage markers specifically when we don't want them to be emitted by codegen. It's only enabled when `sess.mir_opt_level() > 0 && !sess.emit_lifetime_markers()`. Storage markers can be used by codegen backends for their analyses as well. If we weren't emitting them during codegen ever, we wouldn't need to bother removing them here; we could just wait until `RemoveStorageMarkers`.

body:
`StorageDead`s can be used by codegen. I really think this should still include the specific reasoning for why we don't send MIR with `StorageDead`s in cleanup blocks to codegen and why we don't make make cleanup blocks with only `StorageDead`s in them (but do include them for borrowck when there's also `Value` drops). This should also still include the note about how they're used by the MIR coroutine transform.

Also, I mention this elsewhere too, but `ForLint` drops don't produce destructors. They're just for future-compatibility linting. The only thing we get from a cleanup block with `ForLint` drops but no `Value` drops is more future-compat lints in very specific cases.

author: dianne

body: `ForLint` drops don't actually run any code. They turn into `BackwardIncompatibleDropHint` statements marking where a drop will be after a future/edition change, which are used by lint passes to help in issuing compatibility warnings. I wouldn't say it requires cleanup, as such.. I think they're fine to include for more precise linting, but it shouldn't affect correctness if they're gone; we'd just get more false negatives in some very niche use cases.

body:
Well, that's awkward. I took another look at the r-a error too, and I think it and the cargo error are the same thing: a `Drop` local is modified to reference a later-declared non-`Drop` local, then moved so it's dropped before the non-`Drop` local is deallocated. Previously this worked fine. After this change, if there's a function call before the move happens, the non-`Drop` local is `StorageDead` on the call's unwind path before the `Drop` local's destructor is called, even though that's not the order on the happy path. Given that this has come up multiple times in PR CI, I imagine it'd be fairly common in the wild.

I've only looked at the smaller test diffs so far, but I have some comments I'd like addressed before I dig deeper.

Oh, I'm a little confused here. Let me give my reasoning:

I took the original condition:
```
child <= end - 2 (if end <= 3, then hole <= 2, child <= 5, so we skip the loop, so I think we can simplify saturating sub to this)
-> 2*hole + 1 <= end - 2 (applying the invariant child = 2*hole + 1)
-> hole <= (end - 3) / 2
```

I'm using this derived condition `hole <= (end - 3) / 2` in my edit. Is this correct?

author: Saphereye

body:
Yeah, that is correct. However, you can get rid of the separate check for `end <= 3`:
```
end >= 3 && hole.pos() <= (end - 3) / 2
// (x <= y) = (x < y + 1) with integers
end >= 3 && hole.pos() < 1 + (end - 3) / 2
// 1 + (x / 2) = (x + 2) / 2
end >= 3 && hole.pos() < (end - 1) / 2
// if end < 3, this is always false
hole.pos() < (end - 1) / 2
```

Note that the parent of a node can be computed as `(child - 1) / 2`, which is why `hole.pos() < (end - 1) / 2` can be understood as "position is below the parent of `end`"

body:
So, I'm a bit confused by this 😅 We have been running Clippy on `cg_gcc` on CI since January 2025 (https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/pull/135478). So this PR probably doesn't do exactly what you expect it to.

https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/pull/149425 was blocked *exactly* because we run Clippy on cg_gcc in this repository, otherwise it wouldn't fail.

We could ensure that cg_gcc's Clippy version and this repo's Clippy version will stay more up-to-date, or we can change the Clippy configuration in this repo, if needed. But other than that, if we run Clippy on cg_gcc, sometimes the Clippy update *will* break if there are new lints triggered in cg_gcc, that's just how it is. This does not happen so much for the compiler and library, because they only apply a subset of Clippy lints. While `cg_gcc` is configured to `-Dwarnings` in this repository. This was ~~prophesized~~ [expected](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/pull/135478#issuecomment-2591029625).

This is why I dislike random diagnostic changes like this, the added logic partially repeats a piece of logic that normally happens in an entirely different function (`resolve_ident_in_lexical_scope`/`resolve_ident_in_scope_set`).

Also, `foo` may be "not found" in multiple other locations, not just the current module (local variables, unnamed blocks, various preludes).
I guess it's ok to suppress the error if a poisoned module was found in at least one of those location during the search.
And `parent_scope.module.nearest_parent_mod()` will always be in the list.
author: petrochenkov

body: Also, if `compile_error!()` is written in a block, then it will not poison the block module (it doesn't have a `DefId`), but that's probably better than poisoning a wrong module (e.g. parent), which doesn't happen here.

body:
It refers somehow to this [multiclass feature thing](https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/blob/8f36135aea5dfbb5f090ca9ad055094c9913f735/llvm/lib/Target/ARM/ARMFeatures.td#L59). "_D16_SP" is "with only 16 d-registers and no double precision". :shrug: 

Does the Rust vfp4 feature imply double-precision? Who actually understands this zoo of target features? (Or is it maybe documented somewhere? The LLVM source code is not readable unfortunately.)
author: RalfJung

body:
> Does the Rust vfp4 feature imply double-precision

Ah, yes, yes it does. I didn't spot that vfp3/4 are defined with a defm rather than a def so they use the `def ""` record of the multiclass. I've check with an LLVM engineer and I think we should use vfp3d16 instead of `+vfp3,-d32` - the -d32 disables vfp3 (but leaves vfp2 up)

The LLVM dependencies expanded should be (maybe best read bottom to top):
```
vfp4 -> vfp3 + fp16 + vfp4d16 + vfp4sp
vfp4d16 -> vfp3d16 + fp16 + fp64 + vfp4d16sp
vfp4sp -> vfp3sp + fp16 + d32 + vfp4d16sp
vfp4d16sp -> vfp3d16sp + fp16
vfp3 -> vfp2 + vfp3d16 + vfp3sp
vfp3d16 -> vfp2 + fp64 + vfp3d16sp
vfp3sp -> vfp2 + d32 + vfp3d16sp
vfp3d16sp -> vfp2sp
vfp2 -> vfp2sp + fp64
vfp2sp -> fpregs
```
author: adamgemmell

body:
I am not sure we want all that combinatorial explosion in Rust. It would make much more sense to say that `vfp4`  (etc) just means only the d16 part, and then if people want the d32 part they can separately enable that. That is the status quo as well.

IOW, your `vfp3` implies `d32` and that's exactly what we don't want any more, I think. (Also your `vfp2` does not imply `d32` which seems inconsistent.)
author: RalfJung

body:
The list above is just the LLVM features expanded - yes it's inconsistent but it's the way it is for backwards compatibility reasons. I agree we should probably expose something simpler.

I think there's a bunch of tradeoffs with how we could model this in Rust - your solution would trip up many engineers from C who expect `vfp3` to behave the same in both. Arm are target maintainers of a few 32 bit arm targets now so we should update this set of features in the future and look at getting it stabilised.
author: adamgemmell

body: Yeah it's the usual trade-off when the C situation is a mess -- do we copy the same mess for Rust so all Rust people also have to deal with it, or do we do something cleaner in Rust and risk confusing people that deal with both.
author: RalfJung

body:
Left some comments.

In general the changes made so far look relatively minimal and simple. However, currently just moving things to the stdlib's `Cargo.toml` doesn't change the fact that bootstrap can arbitrarily override them (and likely it does override a bunch of them), in CI dist jobs that can have arbitrary bootstrap configuration enabled.

If we wanted to ensure that this won't happen, we should have some sanity check that will simply deny configuring any cargo overrides and rustflags for the stdlib when we are doing a dist build on CI.

body: Cargo forces `-Cembed-bitcode=no` when `lto = false`. The standard library has to be compiled with `lto = false` to support building user code without LTO, but it has to also use `-Cembed-bitcode=yes` to support building user code with LTO. Cargo doesn't have a builtin way to express this specific combination of flags.

body:
It's surprising that rust.optimize controls the profile here only for bootstrap to change most of the [values](https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/blob/fc4c92b64d1e0046b66cbdc747cc1c17af8b35a0/src/cargo/core/profiles.rs#L717) of the dev profile according to bootstrap.toml

rust.optimize=0, everything else default:
```
CARGO_INCREMENTAL="0" CARGO_PROFILE_DEV_DEBUG="0" CARGO_PROFILE_DEV_DEBUG_ASSERTIONS="false" CARGO_PROFILE_DEV_OPT_LEVEL="0" CARGO_PROFILE_DEV_OVERFLOW_CHECKS="false" CARGO_PROFILE_DEV_STRIP="false"
```

At the moment I just created a new dev-dist, but I'm not really sure if this is best here given that compiler_builtins codegen-units and a bunch of other per-package overrides don't apply to the dev profile, and so magically are turned off when rust.optimize is false. To me if bootstrap overrides everything in the profile anyway it should just use one profile.

This obviously doesn't account for visibility meaning if upstream makes a public type with private fields uninhabited it could break downstream.

Is that already considered to be a breaking change due to other features? I see a bunch of uses of `is_uninhabited` in the compiler, so maybe?

Counterexamples: Exhaustiveness checking accounts for visibility at *module* granularity even, `#[repr(transparent)]` no longer permits external types with private fields (well, it's still a future-incompat deny-by-default lint, `repr_transparent_non_zst_fields`).

Edit: This concern probably doesn't really matter actually, it's super theoretical. It's more important that we reject the bad statics.

author: nnethercote

body:
I thought about it a bit and I think for this lint specifically this is fine.

a `static` implies the existence of the value, so there is either
- Some library that provides a value of such type (`extern` `static`)
- Some (accessible) function that returns a value of such type (normal `static`)

In both cases making the value uncostructable would already be a breaking change.

----

Another way to look at this: having a static of an uninhabited type is _unsound_ and leads to UB on basically any use of such static. We shouldn't compile such programs, period.
author: WaffleLapkin

body:
> We shouldn't compile such programs, period.

I do agree.

> having a static of an uninhabited type is unsound and leads to UB on basically any use of such static

Re. supposed *unsoundness* tho, is that really the case? Merely defining such a static isn't insta-UB or is it? And since extern statics are unsafe to reference, no language invariant is broken in safe code, so it's not *unsound*; sure it is UB but that would be the user's fault.

body: It refers to one of `[a, b)` or `(a, b]` where one of the ends are exclusive. In our case, `a..b` is the `[a, b)` right-side half-open range. body:
I'm fairly sure from my tests the sysroot would point at `~/.rustup/toolchains/<toolchain>`.

If I read https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/8202d110516c6bd87cee375e9a9b774db499015c/compiler/rustc_session/src/filesearch.rs#L190 right, it would indeed stirp all the subdirectories.
author: mati865

body: The code below only strips `sess.opts.sysroot.path()`, which only returns the value explicitly passed to `--sysroot` if passed, only returning the fallback sysroot when it isn't passed. https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/8202d110516c6bd87cee375e9a9b774db499015c/compiler/rustc_session/src/config.rs#L1345-L1348 You need `sess.opts.sysroot.all_paths()` to check both.
author: bjorn3

body: Good point, but is `--sysroot` ever passed to rustc when building natively (host == target)?
author: mati865

body: There are legitimate reasons to pass it when building natively. For example miri does for it's copy of the standard library that contains MIR for all functions (though it doesn't need a linker). `cargo build -Zbuild-std` doesn't use `--sysroot` currently, but I think it should pass `--sysroot /dev/null` or something like that to prevent accidentally picking up libraries from the sysroot. The only thing that leads to is lang item conflicts. `--sysroot /dev/null` would give nicer error messages.
author: bjorn3

body: `--sysroot /dev/null` would break all cases of implicit self-contained detection, not just `rust-lld`. I mean, there is no obvious issue caused by this PR, not that there is no general issue with implicit self-contained detection.

body: @FractalFir had a good suggestion: add a new method `is_convergent_safe` to `MirPass`, so we can have a more fine-grained control over which passes run. Whether it defaults to returning true or false is an open question; false would be more conservative (e.g. safer when new passes are added) but more verbose because it's true for many passes.
author: nnethercote

body: @Flakebi: what do you think about this idea?
author: nnethercote

body:
I’m fine with any solution to fix this problem. `is_convergent_safe` sounds like a good idea! I’ll work on that.

I don’t expect everyone working on rustc to know about convergence and GPUs (in fact, I expect the majority to not know about it).
It’s also a topic that requires quite careful handling. Reconvergence guarantees is not a completely solved problem, and there was no discussion in Rust about this as far as I’m aware.
Defaulting to `false` seems like the better choice.
author: Flakebi

body:
Ok, I went through all MIR passes and I’m not so sure about my statement that we want it to default to false anymore.

Many passes are trivial (do only very local or specific transforms), they are fine regarding convergence.
The ones I looked further into are
- SimplifyCfg
- SimplifyConstCondition
- Inline (requires SimplifyCfg)
- InstSimplify
- GVN
- MatchBranchSimplification
- EarlyOtherwiseBranch

**All of them** should be fine regarding convergence. If control flow is changed (like for EarlyOtherwiseBranch), it is only done in simple cases where the affected instructions have no side-effects with respect to convergence.

That means, the only MIR pass that is not convergence safe is `JumpThreading`.

What is your preference for the default of `is_convergence_safe()`?
author: Flakebi

body:
There were some previous related discussions on this, e.g. [here](https://rust-lang.zulipchat.com/#narrow/channel/131828-t-compiler/topic/GPU.20expertise.20in.20the.20team/near/538022883) and [here.](https://rust-lang.zulipchat.com/#narrow/channel/422870-t-compiler.2Fgpgpu-backend/topic/Issue.20with.20JumpThreading.20and.20_syncthreads.28.29) I think there was also one on gh.


cc @saethlin, @RalfJung, who might have opinions about changing the default.
The main problem I see is not your current analysis, but future development, when MIR devs prefer not to have to think about convergent. GPU people with expertise would need to become reviewers for MIR opt PRs, or we risk introducing subtle bugs in Rust GPU code in the future. We don't have that many contributors/reviewers on either side, so adding such a dependency edge might be problematic. 

Since MIR opts are mostly targeting compile time improvements I don't care too strongly about having them on GPU code, since I have a few projects in mind which should bring much bigger compile time improvements. If we get more serious about LIR that opinion would shift, but I don't think that's a focus for now.
For now, a `-Zunsafe-mir-gpu-opts` might flag that runs mir opt minus JumpThreading might be an alternative option to see how much value we even get out of it? In that case MIR can remain ignorant of `convergent` and GPU people who care can maintain this flag and are able to still get the benefits of the safe optimizations.
author: ZuseZ4

body:
MIR semantics currently cannot represent "convergent" operations. (I think that name is confusing and not great in a general-purpose compiler, since it sounds like the opposite of "divergent", which typically means "loops forever".) I have no idea what the semantics of an IR has to look like to support them.

GPU targets decided to add an operation that fundamentally cannot be added to a language without turning it inside out -- this is similar to the "non-temporal" load/store debacle, or "setjmp".

So until we all decide that GPU targets are important enough to overhaul the basics of Rust semantics and everyone writing any kind of semantic analysis or transformation in rustc is expected to take "convergent" operations into account, a bunch of unprincipled hacks is the best we can do.

I really wish hardware vendors would stop assuming that you can just add anything that exists in assembly as a new surface language operation. But no, fundamentally non-compositional operations get added to C and now Rust without any plan and then someone (typically not the people who created the mess) can spend years cleaning it up. :/

So yeah I am entirely fine with sweeping the problem under the rug by disabling all mir-opts. NVidia engineers should have thought about this *before* exposing such operations in languages like C or Rust.

If we go for a `is_convergent_safe` predicate, we're externalizing the cost to anyone adding or modifying MIR passes. At the very least we then need excellent documentation so that someone who never programmed a GPU can figure out whether their pass is "convergent safe". Is there any rigorous, formal semantics of a language with convergent operations that can be used as basis for this?
author: RalfJung

body:
I think the name comes from threads “diverging” (e.g. through an if-else or a loop with different iteration count) and then threads (re-)converging afterwards.
The most extensive document I know about is this one in LLVM: https://llvm.org/docs/ConvergentOperations.html

Such operations exist (at least?) since GLSL 4.0, which is from 2010 and introduced [barrier](https://registry.khronos.org/OpenGL-Refpages/gl4/html/barrier.xhtml).
I’m not completely sure about this, but I think nvidia may have fixed this problem by introducing the `mask` parameter to every convergent operation. The mask specifies the set of threads that should execute the operation together.

> But no, fundamentally non-compositional operations get added to C and now Rust without any plan and then someone (typically not the people who created the mess) can spend years cleaning it up. :/

I think that’s not the expectation. As far as I know, “non-GPU people” in clang and LLVM don’t (need to) care about (re-)convergence. All work done in that space comes from people that run GPUs :)
author: Flakebi

body: I assume you are not one of the people that created the mess so I am thankful that you are helping to clean it up. :)
author: RalfJung

body: I'm still in favor of disabling all MIR opts for targets with convergent operations. I think I'm the one who advocated for that approach, because (similar to Ralf's comment above) I don't trust that we will be able to maintain a correct list of transforms that are convergent-safe. The impact of turning off MIR opts for these targets should be minimal if anything, and the compiler implementation is maintainable. If lacking MIR opts becomes a problem in practice, we can revisit.
author: saethlin

body:
> don't trust that we will be able to maintain a correct list of transforms that are convergent-safe.
Correct me if I am wrong, but all operations which don't affect control flow are divergence-safe. 

In MIR, *to my knowledge*, control flow is only affected by terminators. So any MIR transform which does not change terminators does not affect convergence either. 

We *already* invalidate certain things if control flow is affected.[ `as_mut_preserves_cfg` ]( https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/b53da9908134f77da3bb36e7009ac81f02ee48dc/compiler/rustc_middle/src/mir/basic_blocks.rs#L99)exists for operations which don't change control flow, and we call [`invalidate_cfg_cache`](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/b53da9908134f77da3bb36e7009ac81f02ee48dc/compiler/rustc_middle/src/mir/basic_blocks.rs#L108) if control flow CAN be changed.

So, in my mind, a simple-and-easy to understand notion of `convergence_safe` operations is: operations which don't call `invalidate_cfg_cache`. We *maaaaybe* could even check for that in debug(via an assert in `invalidate_cfg_cache` which checks for `is_gpu_like`). 

This seems like a simple and easy to maintain solution: anybody not calling `invalidate_cfg_cache` when needed would have been introducing bugs anyway. 

Granted, I could be *wrong*... but this feels like the right move.
author: FractalFir

body:
I took a look at LLVM and where it queries convergent information to get a feeling for the impact (ignoring CodeGen and Target specific code).
It comes up in a few places:
- Things related to loops
- Code sinking/hoisting (cannot sink/hoist convergent operations out of/into control-flow)
- GVN/CSE (cannot combine two convergent calls into one)
- JumpThreading/SimplifyCFG (no sinking/hoisting and yes, SimplifyCFG also implements JumpThreading)
- CallSiteSplitting (cannot copy calls into different control-flow)

Given that LLVM has a couple of transforms (1 million lines when ignoring the CodeGen/Target code), that’s not a lot.

Reading through LLVM code, `convergent` is usually not the only condition that gets a pass to abort.
Something that is handled with similar “opaqueness” – also in Rusts’s JumpThreading – is inline assembly.

(Not sure how FractalFir’s comment above would be implemented, but we cannot forbid all cfg changes, ForceInline and SimplifyCfg are required to run.)
author: Flakebi

body:
I see a couple of different ways forward for this, from big to smaller hammer:
1. Disable all mir opts (note: among others, force-inlining, which includes simplify-cfg, cannot be disabled)
1. Disable certain mir passes, all disabled by default (everything but JumpThreading can be enabled)
1. Disable certain mir passes, all enabled by default (only JumpThreading needs to be disabled)
1. Make JumpThreading compatible with convergent operations (i.e. for gpu targets, handle calls like inline assembly in this pass)

Under the premise that we see GPUs/convergent ops like tier-3 targets, where there’s a best effort not to break them, but it’s not particularly bad if a mir opt doesn’t handle convergent correctly,
none of these solutions seems particularly good or bad (except maybe 2, which is lots of code for achieving the same as 3).

There are some comments in mir opts that claim they do optimizations that LLVM is bad at doing, this makes me not wanting to go with 1 (though I am maintaining a GPU target, so I have a rather selective view on this).
Given the information in the comments above, what spectrum of solutions are you all ok with? :)
author: Flakebi

body:
> Disable all mir opts (note: among others, force-inlining, which includes simplify-cfg, cannot be disabled)

You are confusing optimizations with the `MirPass` implementors. Not all of the MIR transformations are optimizations. Optimizations are always optional. You can disable all optimizations, for example Miri does this by default by setting `-Zmir-opt-level=0`.
author: saethlin

body: That said, it seems wrong that force-inlining would be enabled for Miri. But that's a separate discussion: https://github.com/rust-lang/unsafe-code-guidelines/issues/597.
author: RalfJung

body:
Regarding this PR, I think the goal should be to make it so that people working on MIR transforms (adding new ones or adjusting/extending existing ones) generally should not have to think about convergent operations. So this isn't just about what exactly a pass does today, but also about likely extensions to the pass that might occur in the future.

Ultimately this is up to @rust-lang/wg-mir-opt.
author: RalfJung

body:
You can have macros expand to comments to reduce duplication here. 

```rust
#[doc = macro_exanding_to_string!()]
```

So a common recipe is to put text in a separate `.md` document and then e.g. `include_str!("intrinsic_is_convergent.md")` it into the doc comment. 

I suspect  that'll be easier to maintain?

body: We should check the intended and actual semantics of this carefully, given all the recent discussion around the semantics of min and max (see https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/149537 and also [LLVM discussions](https://discourse.llvm.org/t/rfc-a-consistent-set-of-semantics-for-the-floating-point-minimum-and-maximum-operations/89006)). In particular, IEEE-754 `maxNum` is *not* what we do for `$float::max` -- do we really want a different semantics here, and do we really *have* a different semantics here?
author: RalfJung

body: This has the same concern as `max`.
author: RalfJung

body: In fact what we currently implement in the interpreter is NOT IEEE-754 maxNum. It is the same as ``$float::max``, which is a mix of IEEE 754-2008 maxNum and IEEE 754-2019 maximumNumber. I don't know if that's what the users of this operation want, and I don't know what the codegen backends are doing.
author: RalfJung

body: More specifically, const-stabilization is in progress at https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/pull/148052.
author: RalfJung

body: Also see https://github.com/rust-lang/stdarch/issues/1971.
author: RalfJung

body: ah ok, then I think it would be better to wait until that lands first
author: sayantn

body: I am ok with not doing it now, we don't use it in stdarch anyway
author: sayantn

body: I see, LLVM is also very weird in this scenario. It ways `llvm.vector.reduce.fmax` behaves like an ordered reduction, with the operation being `llvm.maxnum` -- but this doesn't align exactly with IEEE `maxNum`. But in x86 stdarch, we only use it for integers (we explicitly avoid using any of the `reduce` intrinsics in x86 stdarch because the semantics and ordering of the operations don't match exactly with x86)

body:
i am not happy of having two error types representing roughly the same error. using only `UnknownFeature` here means we need to be able to refer to `Some<Ident>` when creating the suggestion, which is (as far as i can tell) not a thing. i would love to be able to do the following:

```rust
#[derive(Diagnostic)]
#[diag(passes_unknown_feature, code = E0635)]
pub(crate) struct UnknownFeature {
    #[primary_span]
    pub span: Span,
    pub feature: Symbol,
    #[suggestion(
        style = "verbose",
        code = "{actual_name}",
        applicability = "maybe-incorrect"
    )]
    pub actual_name: Option<(Span, Symbol)>,
}
```

body:
Looks good to me; thanks! One last request: could you fix the PR and commit descriptions? Specifically,

> Any `ExprKind::ConstBlock` inside a pattern now lowers to `PatKind::Err`, consistent with how other invalid pattern expressions are handled.

This isn't the case. Invalid expression patterns lower to `PatKind::Expr` of `PatExprKind::Lit` representing a `LitKind::Err`. This doesn't need to be in the PR description, but I'd like to make sure whatever details are there are accurate. ^^

I think it'd be fine without that sentence, but it also wouldn't hurt to keep the detail about being consistent with how other invalid pattern expressions are handled. I'll leave the particulars up to you.

> - Removed `Pat::ConstBlockPat` from Rust Analyzer.

This is no longer part of the PR.

> - Cleaned up Clippy and other tools.

No other tools needed cleanup. And this is just a nit, but I think the clippy change could count as part of removing `PatExprKind::ConstBlock` from the HIR; there shouldn't be any other cleanup to clippy.

body:
Thanks for looking into this. I like the attempt and the careful measurements. Unfortunately it doesn't seem to help full-compiler performance, as measured by rust-timer, and slightly regresses a few benchmarks. Lexing is a really small component of overall compilation time, and it's already been micro-optimized quite a bit, so it's hard for it have much effect on performance. I suspect adding `#[inline]` to all those functions had the biggest effect and caused the regressions.

*[View changes since this review](https://triagebot.infra.rust-lang.org/gh-changes-since/rust-lang/rust/149689/66428d92bec337ed4785d695d0127276a482278c..ef2fa3b4ca1fb712cd4287928879787b5ff850d5)*

body: This needs to check for overflow, otherwise it's unsound to do unchecked `ptr.add`s and `copy_nonoverlapping`s below.
author: hanna-kruppe

body:
You're right, I forgot about the case where multiple `str`s reuse the same memory by having their addresses overlap, and was operating under the assumption all arguments were disjoint, in which case overflow would be impossible.

What do you recommend doing in the overflow case? just panicing with a generic message? triggering the oom handler to preserve behavior (as that's what the generic implementation would do in the overflow case)?
author: lolbinarycat

body: Maybe just saturate and let `reserve` panic from capacity overflow, just like the previous `push_str` calls would have? I don't know off-hand if there's any significant benefit to actual overflow check vs. saturation in this context.
author: hanna-kruppe

body:
Maybe
```suggestion
#[cfg_attr(no_global_oom_handling, allow(dead_code))]
```
author: bjorn3

body:
It's always a good idea to `#[expect]` these sort of things so that people know when they become unnecessary.
```suggestion
#[cfg_attr(no_global_oom_handling, expect(dead_code))]
```
author: joboet

body:
```suggestion
    /// SAFETY: When calling `<S as AsRef<str>::as_ref()` multiple times, the same value must be returned.
```
at first I thought you were talking about a consistent trait impl needing to apply in the face of specialization.
author: bjorn3

body: This is unsound – it's possible to pass the same string multiple times (or overlapping strings), in which case this sum will saturate. But in that case, the capacity will not suffice here.
author: joboet

body: Is this necessary for performance? Otherwise, I'd rather you use a `loop` and `return` instead of testing `repeat`.
author: joboet

body: `reserve` will abort the program with an OOM error in that case, no?
author: lolbinarycat

body: Yes, esp since no single allocation can be `isize::MAX` bytes large, reserve is guaranteed to fail if the sum saturates (in this case it will panic when it tries to calculate the new layout of the allocation, not OOM).
author: RustyYato

One thing I'm wondering is if we need to track the remapped working directory in addition to the local working directory? Creating the remapped working directory is quite awkward and I'm not sure that's even useful.
author: Urgau

author: bjorn3

Yes, they are already tracked in `Config::remap_path_prefix` and `UnstableOptions::remap_path_scopes`.

I will therefor remove the unnecessary remapping.
author: Urgau

Instead of duplicating what `visit_generics` is doing & manually visiting the `after_where_clause`, could you instead please

1. remove the existing `visit_generics` implementation entirely (the entire `fn`)
2. remove `check_ty_alias_after_where_clause` again
3. instead, explicitly implement `visit_where_predicate_kind` so it's calling `check_late_bound_lifetime_defs` with the expected arguments, followed by `visit::walk_where_predicate_kind(…)` to continue the recursion
   * this should work because we automatically visit both before & after where clauses (due to `derive(Walkable)`) (and if that doesn't happen, then that's a bug)
author: fmease

body:
> Now I'm slightly worried that there might be other regressions caused by PR #147713 due to visitors only overriding `visit_generics` to visit where predicates instead of `visit_where_predicate{,_kind}` (they wouldn't really be at fault since the rug (some hidden assumptions) was pulled under them).

I did a search and found that very few places override `visit_generics`. Those that do either already call `visit_where_predicate` directly or just use the default walker , so they still reach `visit_where_predicate_kind`.

@rustbot ready

body:
This is a breaking change to stable Rust's grammar. This is not acceptable without T-lang involvement and a crater run.

Moreover this renders the grammar quite inconsistent by syntactically rejecting bounds on higher-ranked type parameters but syntactically permitting them on higher-ranked lifetime parameters.

author: scottmcm

body:
I think default type params can lead to frustrating and confusing bugs, particularly silent errors that work for upstream but break downstream. I have had several of these when refactoring data structures to use the unstable Allocator trait. A lint that demands all generic parameters be provided explicitly sounds too complex for the AST-based approach I believe clippy is limited to.

One particular concern I have with the `u8` default is assuming that slice length in elements is always equivalent to the number of bytes, which could interact poorly with the `unsafe` set_init() method. I can very easily imagine the default leading to an upstream crate later deciding they want to support more types, and in the process failing to multiply by `size_of::<T>()`. I see alignment being potentially even trickier.

I do see #150129 potentially improving this, but I believe the concern remains for the `BorrowedCursor` struct.

It seems prudent to have tests with a `T` that isn't the same size as `u8` to ensure that there are no byte-size vs. slice-length issues.

Similarly, it would be nice to have some test for non-`Copy` `T`. Since the `BorrowedBuf` doesn't own any `T` then no special drop handling is necessary. Is it worth calling this out? I seem to remember in the discussion of doing this that people thought that this could be done for only `T: Copy` presumably because of drop checking.

body: Yeah I already mentioned that above -- this needs to become a lang item so the compiler can invoke it to implement the `Abort` MIR terminator and other "native" aborts.
author: RalfJung

body:
I think that's categorically different. The compiler inserts a single byte trap in some places where a code path is unreachable. That seems a bit unnecessary but fine as a debugging aid. It's only one byte.

`core::intrinsic::abort`  is different. It's used in `core` where we would use `std::panic::abort` in `std`, except that obviously that's unavailable.
author: ChrisDenton

body: I think it would be very strange if some aborts behave different from others. I am not talking about unreachable code. "Native" aborts occur, for instance, when code unwinds out of a `drop`, or out of an `extern "C"`.
author: RalfJung

body:
We already have different aborts with the core and std difference I mentioned. Should the compiler insert calls to `abort()` on unix platforms?

I do think the compiler misnames what should be called trap.
author: ChrisDenton

body:
We already have different aborts with the core and std difference I mentioned. Should the compiler insert calls to `abort()` on unix platforms?

I do think the compiler misnames what should be called trap.
author: ChrisDenton

body:
So far we've had a fairly clear line -- everything that can't rely on std does the "simple" `abort`; std calls the libc function. As-is this PR makes things messy because the line is now different for different OSes.

We shouldn't have an abort intrinsic and an abort terminator that do different things. There's absolutely no reason to do that either. Lang items aren't expensive or complicated. We have plenty of lang items already exactly for situations like this ("native" operations being implemented in libcore).
author: RalfJung

body:
The only reason for the difference between core and std is that core cannot depend on libc (or equivalent). But that's not an issue in this specific case. There's simply no motivation for them to be different.

When we move things into core, we have to change which panic is used even in cases where it's still used through std. This is not for a principled reason. It's just a technical issue with the way core and std are currently split. If we knew `libc::abort` was available we'd use it.
author: ChrisDenton

body:
> When we move things into core, we have to change which panic is used even in cases where it's still used through std. 

You mean "which abort"? Panics always go through the panic handler so it doesn't matter where it comes from.

There's no *downside* to using a lang item, is there? Why are you opposing that proposal?
author: RalfJung

body: I'm not opposing it but the downside is unnecessary complexity. If this can simply be a library implementation then why not? It doesn't have any special semantics that aren't already expressible.
author: ChrisDenton

body: :shrug: I don't think it is much complexity, and I don't think it is unnecessary.
author: RalfJung

body:
> For example, if the goal is to change what abort does, should https://doc.rust-lang.org/nightly/nightly-rustc/rustc_codegen_llvm/builder/struct.GenericBuilder.html#method.abort be the thing that changes instead so that any uses of abort in cg_ssa also do a __fastfail?

Doing that would replace every single `ud2` instruction of two bytes with a seven byte instruction sequence, and I doubt LLVM would be allowed / smart enough to optimize out the lang item call like it does with unneeded `llvm.trap` instructions.

body:
It might compile but the generated asm is definitely incorrect, and this may cause LLVM assert failures in the future even if it doesn't today.

The reason xmm supports multiple sizes is because fundamentally xmm0, ymm0 and zmm0 are just different names for the same register. This is not the case for amdgpu since v0 and v[0:1] are very distinct, for example the latter overlaps v1 but the former doesn't. So these really need to be separate register classes.
author: Amanieu

body:
Considering the sheer variety of register sizes, I think that this should be represented like this instead:
```rust
enum AmdgpuInlineAsmRegClass {
    Sgpr(u16),
    Vgpr(u16),
}
```

Then you would manually implement the relevant methods instead of using the `def_reg_class` macro.

You will need to add validation during `parse` to ensure that the register size is valid: it must be a multiple of 32, with an exception for `{sv}gpr16` which represents half of a normal register.

author: fmease

body:
There's something a lot bigger here, which is why I didn't take the time to look at this PR until now.

At its core, a lot of code in the compiler assumes that if a type is well-formed, so are its fields. This is simply not true, neither currently nor in general:
- currently there's https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/149283
- in general, there's https://github.com/lcnr/random-rust-snippets/issues/23

This is separate to the issue in this PR, where the struct itself is not well-formed and we don't emit an error for it. These we could handle by changing `normalize_erasing_regions` to emit a `span_delayed_bug`, returning an `ty::Error` instead. Doing that is kind of whatever however if the issue also just exists for otherwise well-formed programs.

Outside of codegen, using `normalize_erasing_regions` can pretty much always go wrong. Quickly looking through to source, the following additional calls to `normalize_erasing_regions` may also result in reachable ICE
- https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/e951f470d76febcc6f0a5b409c509eb77450a336/compiler/rustc_middle/src/ty/layout.rs#L545 (we recur into struct fields)
- https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/e951f470d76febcc6f0a5b409c509eb77450a336/compiler/rustc_middle/src/ty/layout.rs#L906 (should ICE for reference to alias as struct field)
- pretty much all (indirect) uses of `normalize_erasing_regions` in CTFE and MIR opts can result in ICE as long as they happen in generic bodies 
- using `normalize_erasing_regions` in borrowck for the not explicitly mentioned fields of `Foo { ..Default::default() }` expressions should ICE
- using `normalize_erasing_regions` in `resolve_instance_raw` ICEs mir opts and CTFE using it

I would kind of like to figure out a more general strategy here instead of fixing these issues as they crop up. Anyways, fine with this PR for now, but want to start a T-types discussion about this and think about a general solution here.

*[View changes since this review](https://triagebot.infra.rust-lang.org/gh-changes-since/rust-lang/rust/149800/018d26972e523b8d28f9579056185a1713949dfd..badc3d28487c02950e876c3e8fe6edf8b926d78d)*
author: lcnr

body: can you instead replace the `normalize_erasing_regions` call in `has_significant_drop` with its `try_` variant. r=me after.

body: Done! I've moved the fix to `has_significant_drop` itself, replacing `normalize_erasing_regions` with `try_normalize_erasing_regions` and returning `false` when normalization fails.

For stuff like this I typically prefer a dummy comment over `#[rustfmt::skip]`.

> Does "Inclusion" have any particular meaning? I'm not sure what it's trying to communicate.

In my mind I consider query dependency graph to be analogous to lattice of open sets in a topological space. As one open set could include another open set, dependent query includes dependency query.

I wanted to ask if there could be a better name, since this inclusion stuck to me and now I can't think of anything fitting myself.

body: As I mentioned in my [original PR](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/pull/149859) this approach leads to deadlocks and is unacceptable. The "we just need to prevent other threads from acquiring it while printing our dbg!" part is impossible. For example when you write `dbg!(f())` then `f()` may block until another thread does some task (e.g. could be as simple and innocent as `dbg!("foo", chan.recv())`) . And that other task may also call `dbg!`, at which point you've deadlocked.
author: orlp

body:
I guess that's true, though that's already the case for the formatting itself (Debug impls using dbg! can lead to deadlocks today), right? In order to avoid it we'd need to serialize to a temporary buffer before writing to stderr (perhaps optionally -- if locking fails -- but still).

That also implies a change in behavior if we land this PR: today `dbg!("foo", chan.recv())` prints foo and then waits, whereas with this PR we'd only print foo after both are complete, right? I'm not sure that's an improvement, i.e., it's not obvious that we *should* fix the issue. Do we have a T-libs-api decision somewhere on what the behavior of multi-expression dbg! should be with regards to concurrency?
author: Mark-Simulacrum

body:
> Debug impls using dbg! can lead to deadlocks today

Only if the `Debug` impl causes `dbg!` to be called on another thread, which is rather unusual, but in theory yes. But I don't think it's a fair comparison at all, the situation you describe is incredibly niche, whereas my example can be trivially caused if for example you use `rayon` to parallelize some computation you're debugging.

> That also implies a change in behavior if we land this PR: today dbg!("foo", chan.recv()) prints foo and then waits, whereas with this PR we'd only print foo after both are complete, right? 

Yes. I honestly think the old behavior is more surprising than this new behavior, and trivial to fix if undesirable. On the other hand torn multi-expression `dbg!` statements can be *very* confusing, and it is 'impossible' to fix (you can wrap the entire thing in a tuple but that also changes the output, or write your own macro of course which defeats the point of quick debugging).

> Do we have a T-libs-api decision somewhere on what the behavior of multi-expression dbg! should be with regards to concurrency?

Not that I'm aware of. You can nominate the issue if you feel this change is controversial.
author: orlp

body: ## Pull request overview This PR adds EFIAPI (UEFI calling convention) support for the LoongArch64 architecture by extending the ABI mapping infrastructure. This enables Rust code targeting LoongArch64 to use `extern "efiapi"` function declarations, which is essential for interfacing with UEFI firmware on LoongArch64 systems. **Key Changes:** - Added `LoongArch64` variant to the internal `ArchKind` enum - Mapped `Arch::LoongArch64` to `ArchKind::LoongArch64` in the architecture detection logic - Extended the EFIAPI pattern match to include LoongArch64, mapping it to `CanonAbi::C` (standard C calling convention) --- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. Learn how to get started. *[View changes since this review](https://triagebot.infra.rust-lang.org/gh-changes-since/rust-lang/rust/149879/f5209000832c9d3bc29c91f4daef4ca9f28dc797..903dcf84b975453a6537760858a0164fe8f30635)*
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: The inline assembly clobber ABI support for LoongArch64 should include "efiapi" to be consistent with other architectures that support EFIAPI. Currently, the inline asm parser at `compiler/rustc_target/src/asm/mod.rs:999-1002` only accepts "C" and "system" for LoongArch64, but does not include "efiapi" like AArch64, ARM, RISC-V, and x86 architectures do. This means users cannot use `asm!("", clobber_abi("efiapi"))` on LoongArch64 even though `extern "efiapi"` is now supported.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:

body:
Good question. My understanding is that [CC-BY-4.0](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/) is quite permissive, primarily needing attribution. The embedded SVG in the output includes a comment with all of the license and copyright information. According to [FontAwesome's documentation](https://fontawesome.com/license/free#attribution), that's all that's needed to comply.

Some other details for clarity:

- Previously mdbook was using FontAwesome 4.7 which was licensed under [SIL OFL 1.1](http://scripts.sil.org/OFL) and MIT. That was untracked in our system.
- font-awesome-as-a-crate is maintained by the Rust project in https://github.com/rust-lang/docs.rs.

body: ## Pull request overview This PR updates the `rustc` manpage to correctly document that the `-O` flag is equivalent to `-C opt-level=3` (not `opt-level=2`), aligning it with the output of `rustc --help`. Additionally, it introduces a Python maintenance script to automate keeping this documentation synchronized with the compiler's behavior. **Key Changes:** - Corrected the `-O` flag documentation in the rustc manpage from opt-level=2 to opt-level=3 - Added a comprehensive Python script with dry-run mode, colored diff output, automatic backups, and flexible configuration options ### Reviewed changes Copilot reviewed 2 out of 2 changed files in this pull request and generated 9 comments. | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | `src/doc/man/rustc.1` | Updates the `-O` flag documentation to correctly indicate it's equivalent to `-C opt-level=3` | | `src/tools/update-rustc-man-opt-level.py` | New maintenance script that parses `rustc --help` output and updates the manpage accordingly, with safety features like dry-run mode and timestamped backups | --- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. Learn how to get started. *[View changes since this review](https://triagebot.infra.rust-lang.org/gh-changes-since/rust-lang/rust/149899/5b150d238fbd4fe7bc2cd3140d8e6fb4406099fa..2710c3a837f3c6ea740f9038716daf5c2d5f237b)*
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer
copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: The function signature uses `Tuple` from the typing module. In Python 3.9+, the built-in `tuple` type can be used directly for type hints (PEP 585). Since the script uses `from __future__ import annotations` (line 15) which enables postponed evaluation of annotations, you can use lowercase `tuple[str, int]` instead of `Tuple[str, int]` for better consistency with modern Python style.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: The error message on line 175 could be more helpful by distinguishing between the two cases: (1) manpage is already up-to-date with the correct opt-level, and (2) the -O entry pattern wasn't found in the manpage. Currently both scenarios produce the same message, making troubleshooting more difficult. Consider tracking whether any "Equivalent to" sentences with opt-level were found at all, regardless of whether they needed updating.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
The regex pattern contains an unnecessary escape sequence `\\-` in the character class. In the pattern `r'-O[^\n]*opt(?:\\-)?level\s*=\s*(\d+)'`, the `(?:\\-)?` part is attempting to match an optional hyphen in "opt-level". However, the backslash escape is only needed in the replacement context (for manpage formatting), not when parsing plain text output from `rustc --help`. The pattern should use `(?:-)?` instead to match an optional literal hyphen.
```suggestion
    m = re.search(r'-O[^\n]*opt(?:-)?level\s*=\s*(\d+)', help_text, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
```
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
The regex on line 78 uses an overly broad limit of `{0,800}` characters for the sentence content between "Equivalent to" and the period. This could potentially match across multiple unrelated entries in the manpage. A more conservative limit (e.g., 200 characters) would be safer and still accommodate the expected sentence length while reducing false positives.
```suggestion
    sentence_pattern = re.compile(r'Equivalent to([^\n\.]{0,200}?)\.', flags=re.IGNORECASE)
```
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
The replacement logic on line 106 uses `count=1` to replace only the first digit in the sentence. However, this could be problematic if the sentence structure changes to include other numbers before the opt-level value (e.g., "Equivalent to -C opt-level=3 (level 3 optimization)"). While unlikely, a more robust approach would be to specifically target the digit immediately following "opt-level=" or use the matched number's position from line 87-90.
```suggestion
        # Replace only the number immediately following "opt-level=" (or "opt\-level=")
        new_sentence = re.sub(
            r'(opt(?:\\-)?level\s*=\s*)\d+',
            r'\g<1>{}'.format(new_level),
            sentence,
            count=1,
        )
```
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
Lines 97-103 check for various patterns to detect the "-O" flag in the lookback window, but they don't use an early exit pattern. The condition uses `not (A or B or C or D or E)` which means all patterns must be checked even if the first one matches. Consider restructuring to `if not any([...])` or using early return/continue logic for better readability and potential performance improvement.
```suggestion
        if not any([
            re.search(r'(^|\n)\s*-O\b', window),
            re.search(r'\\fB\\-?O\\fR', window),
            re.search(r'\\-O\b', window),
            re.search(r'\.B\s+-O\b', window),
            re.search(r'\\fB-?O\\fP', window),
        ]):
```
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
The backup timestamp format on line 135 uses `%Y%m%dT%H%M%S` which only includes precision to seconds. If the script is run multiple times within the same second (e.g., in automated tests or rapid iterations), subsequent backups would overwrite earlier ones. Consider adding microseconds (`%f`) or using a different conflict resolution strategy (e.g., checking if backup exists and adding a counter).
```suggestion
    ts = datetime.datetime.now().strftime("%Y%m%dT%H%M%S%f")
```
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
The function extracts the first number found in the sentence (line 87-90) and checks if it equals new_level to skip replacement (line 91-92). However, it then uses a generic regex substitution (line 106) that replaces the first digit in the entire sentence. If the sentence structure is unusual and contains digits before "opt-level=", this could cause a mismatch between what is checked and what is replaced. Consider using the span information from num_match to ensure the same number is replaced.
```suggestion
        # Replace the specific number matched by num_match
        num_start, num_end = num_match.span(1)
        new_sentence = sentence[:num_start] + str(new_level) + sentence[num_end:]
```
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
Instead of unconditionally suggesting a qualified path, IINM you should be able to take advantage of *path trimming* (that's a machinery that shortens paths according to what's in scope / imported).

```suggestion
                    format!("{path} {{ start: {}, end: {} }}", start_name, end_name),
```

where `path` is `tcx.def_path_str(range_struct)` where `range_struct` comes from `tcx.lang_items().range_struct()` (for `RangeEnd::Excluded`; it should he `range_inclusive_struct()` for `RangeEnd::Included(RangeSyntax::DotDotEq)`) if it's `Some(_)` (bail out if it's `None`).

I hope this works in `rustc_resolve` 🤞. If these queries hang, dead-lock or crash, then they obv can't be used in the resolver since the info isn't available yet. If it comes to that, there might be alternative `Resolver` APIs you could use, not sure. Check what other resolver code is doing about lang items.
author: fmease

body: As alluded to in another review comment of mine, you can't ignore the `RangeEnd` of the `PatKind::Range`, it's crucial for suggesting the right struct!
author: fmease

body: It would be very nice to also handle the open ranges, `RangeTo` (`..b`) and `RangeFrom` (`a..`) & suggest the appropriate structs.
author: fmease

body:
```suggestion
                    "if you meant to destructure a range, use a struct pattern",
```

or

```suggestion
                    "if you meant to destructure a `{name}`, use a struct pattern",
```

where `name` is `tcx.item_name(range_struct)` (see other comments about accounting for `RangeInclusive` & open ranges). The query `item_name` might not work in `rustc_resolve` (it might hang or crash since the data isn't available) but then there should be alternative `Resolver` APIs for obtaining that.
author: fmease

I tried implementing path trimming using `tcx.lang_items()` and `def_path_str` as suggested, but it caused a cycle detected [E0391] error exactly as you warned (since resolution info isn't ready yet).

Instead, I implemented a safe manual check using `self.resolve_path.` It checks if the short name (e.g., Range) is visible in the current scope. If it is, I use it; otherwise, I fall back to the fully qualified path `(std::ops::Range).` Tests confirm this works correctly!

author: traviscross

body:
We have similar checks for unreachable match arms, so it can't be that bad, right?

I'm really concerned that this lint in its current form is just misleading. I think a user that sees `#![deny(unreachable_cfg_select_predicates)` will assume that rust will detect all unreachable predicates (since rust is normally reliable like that), and then will not notice that there is an unreachable predicate.
author: WaffleLapkin

body:
> We have similar checks for unreachable match arms, so it can't be that bad, right?

It seems feasible, but it really is adding a SMT solver, so, not straightforward. 

https://doc.rust-lang.org/beta/nightly-rustc/rustc_hir/attrs/data_structures/enum.CfgEntry.html

We currently store 

```rust
pub enum CfgEntry {
    All(ThinVec<CfgEntry>, Span),
    Any(ThinVec<CfgEntry>, Span),
    Not(Box<CfgEntry>, Span),
    Bool(bool, Span),
    NameValue {
        name: Symbol,
        name_span: Span,
        value: Option<(Symbol, Span)>,
        span: Span,
    },
    Version(Option<RustcVersion>, Span),
}
```

Meaning that e.g. for `target_endian` we'd need to encode that covering big/little is exhaustive. We need target feature implications to be taken into account, and cargo feature implication to be taken into account. This all seems useful, but it's complex. I'd rather not block `cfg_select!` on it.

@traviscross do you have more details of what T-lang had in mind here (and whether we want to block `cfg_select!` itself on that?
author: folkertdev

body: Unfortunately, I do not have more details on hand.  I've noted the question, though, and we'll cover this when we pick up the nomination on https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/pull/149783.
author: traviscross

body: Question for you.  I've been working on a minimal SAT solver that fits in around 600 lines of Rust code (while still being impressively fast).  Let's say we had this.  How much of the problem is that, and how much of the problem is encoding the needed invariants in CNF?  I.e., if we had a good solver, is this still hard due to e.g. not knowing what we need to know in the right place to add the clauses, needing hard circuits such as for string operations, etc.?  Or, if we had a solver, would it be straightforward to encode the problem in CNF?
author: traviscross

body:
I honestly have no idea, this is not really my area of expertise.

The `NameValue` variant might need some design around when that is exhaustive. `Version` seems hard, maybe that is just never exhaustive?
author: folkertdev
traviscross

body:
> The `NameValue` variant might need some design around when that is exhaustive.

Yes, and we'd want to think about what we expect of the interaction with `--cfg-check`.

> `Version` seems hard, maybe that is just never exhaustive?

Sounds right. We'd give each version a variable, then assert that at most one of these can be true.

body:
This looks like it will be `false` for `&(*local_ref).field`, which is not what we want.

Generally, I am not convinced by the implementation approach. This adds a very syntactic check to one specific operation that constructs references, but it's far from clear that this is enough. For instance, the code could take a raw pointer to a static, store it in a `[*const T; 1]`, and transmute that to `[&T; 1]` -- then you would never see a (re)borrow of a reference but we still have the same underlying unsoundness.
author: RalfJung

body:
> For instance, 

I guess we'd say that the unsafe code needed to construct references this way is unsound... hm. Maybe that is fair but it needs good documentation.

Also, what you implemented is somewhat different from what I sketched, not sure if that is deliberate. You seem to be trying to catch all `&expr` where `expr` evaluates to a pointer that points to a static. The original plan was just to catch all `&STATIC`, i.e. it really syntactically has  to have that shape.

In particular, your approach would seem to reject this:
```rust
static X: &Never = weird(unsafe { &*&raw const X });
```
I'm not sure that's a good idea. It's unprincipled: it's still possible to write unsafe code that evades your checks, so yo haven't gained any new guarantees. I would rather have a very clear line for which code gets accepted and which gets rejected.
author: RalfJung

body: I don't quite understand what you mean by this. However, it all seems an artifact of trying to catch cases where the reference points to a static, rather than just the specific syntactic pattern `&STATIC`.
author: RalfJung

body:
Yeah, I’m not a fan of this implementation either (I’ll elaborate a bit more in my next comment 😅).
But this doesn't reject `static X: &Never = weird(unsafe { &*&raw const X });` because `is_reborrow_of_ref` is false for that case.

But everything else is exactly as you described - e.g., field projections - so I’ll try the other approach.

author: workingjubilee

body:
Thanks for the review!

Some context on the internal workings:
1. On the CPU side, a program passes arguments to a kernel
2. The “API” takes these arguments and writes them into GPU memory
3. The kernel on the GPU gets a pointer to this memory
4. When the kernel accesses arguments, it reads from this memory

(I think nvidia and amd work the same here. I’m not too familiar with nvidia, but this seems to suggest so: https://github.com/Rust-GPU/rust-cuda/blob/44c44baf6fb738d5ffec25aac5db8af02514e890/crates/rustc_codegen_nvvm/src/abi.rs#L60)

So, number of arguments or size of arguments doesn’t really matter, it’s all memory anyways.
And, we could make struct arguments work (maybe, I didn’t look into the details), but Rust would need to take them by value, currently it changes them to pass by pointer.

*[View changes since this review](https://triagebot.infra.rust-lang.org/gh-changes-since/rust-lang/rust/149991/68f11a11b666b36137374b6e54fc0d013a3b26cf..01f1e1b7c8b124da8f9ad82224937ba7cf93d516)*
author: Flakebi

body:
It’s vague because there’s so many APIs :smile:
A gpu-kernel must be loaded and launched through cuda/hip/sycl/hsa or any wrapper around these APIs.
Driver is often used in the graphics space, but I don’t read it often when it comes to GPGPU.
The kernel loader is also part of cuda/hip/…, but not exactly the piece that launches (as in, for launching a process on the CPU, one doesn’t call ld, but the kernel, even though ld is involved).

The `must` is definitely better, I’ll also remove the `only`.

I think the distinctive part is, only the (GPU) hardware is allowed to start gpu-kernels, they must not be called (from anywhere or anyone).
I guess it’s similar to _start on CPUs?
Note: Launching a kernel is not really a call, it’s 1-to-n – launching n instances of the kernel – and it’s async/doesn’t return.
Maybe `must be launched on the GPU by the hardware` is better?

(Technically, one can call the _start function on a CPU, similarly one can “call” a gpu-kernel on the GPU, it’s just an address pointing to instructions, but one cannot use the gpu-kernel ABI in the call instruction.)
author: Flakebi

body:
It shouldn’t be called from C or the CPU either ;)
See also my other comment, I think nobody can call a gpu-kernel, it can only be “launched” (which is a different thing!).
author: Flakebi

body: Yes, if someone calls their kernel `malloc` and we unmangle it, funny things will happen.
author: Flakebi

body: :+1: I’ll call it ABI instead of calling convention, it seems like Rust calls it ABI in most places. (Unless there’s a difference I’m missing)
author: Flakebi

body:
I would suggest `must be launched on the GPU by the runtime`. 

I think that's somewhat standard even though it's both quite a big term, and there's a slight ambiguity related to CUDA calling something the "Driver API" as opposed to the "Runtime API".

body:
> Some context on the internal workings:
>    1. On the CPU side, a program passes arguments to a kernel
>    1. The “API” takes these arguments and writes them into GPU memory
>    1. The kernel on the GPU gets a pointer to this memory
>    1. When the kernel accesses arguments, it reads from this memory

Ah, yeah. I know of the general idea here, though I am foggy on specifics in many cases.

And even in some hypothetical where the driver isn't writing them into the GPU memory, it would have to invoke some dark magic on the GPU to put it directly into registers anyways, which is just saying "did you know: some computer hardware has memory-mapped registers?"

( I only am making that comment because I vaguely remember one GPU driver involving something similar. )

At some point we are left with doing codegen to accept arguments and we can expect that to have some target-specific nuances, even if it's just stack alignment.

For structs, I'd rather we avoid thinking too hard about the struct question for cases where they aren't just `repr(transparent)` primitives until we have tinkered with things a bit more. If `repr(C)` structs are widely supported and fully intentionally that could be worth tackling.

*[View changes since this review](https://triagebot.infra.rust-lang.org/gh-changes-since/rust-lang/rust/149991/d0835adc4e114eac911150b3692b830b5583223a..6868f66e988848a7f7eeae345ce845f4a2b227b6)*

I do not consider that required, but I thought I should note it because this seems like a sorta-classic `..super_fold..` case, where we want to only consider the "true" type instead of binders and such: https://rustc-dev-guide.rust-lang.org/ty-fold.html

body:
As I see it, this is unrelated to the entire discussion above.

Yes, with current Rust you can not name a `DropGuard` that holds a closure, without coercing that closure into a function pointer (which only works if that closure doesn't capture anything).

However, `DropGuard::new` as this PR has it does not always return a `DropGuard<(), UnitFn<fn()>>`. If type inference doesn't force the passed `FnOnce` to be coerced to a `fn` pointer, it won't be.

`DropGuard::new` _only_ gets rid of the weird unit parameter, that's it. And the default for `F` _only_ allows naming the type of `DropGuard` obtained from `DropGuard::new` (at the expense of the function pointer indirection), nothing more or less.

author: tisonkun

body:
I’m not sure whether this change carries its weight. It makes the main intended use case of this API harder by making main constructor harder to remember. `DropGuard::new` is pretty obvious, `DropGuard::with_value` does not have any precedent in the stdlib, so people will need to either memorize it or look it up:

```rust
let _guard = DropGuard::new(x, |x| ..);        // ← current
let _guard = DropGuard::with_value(x, |x| ..); // ← proposed
```

I would like to see evidence presented that constructing `DropGuard` without any values is so common that we would want to make it the default. In `rust-lang/rust` we have a total of 42 references of the `DropGuard` pattern, of which 7 don’t take any arguments ([ref](https://github.com/search?q=repo%3Arust-lang%2Frust+%28%22struct+withdrop%22+OR+dropguard+OR+%22struct+defer%22+OR+%22panicguard%22+OR+%22dropbomb+OR+%22attrguard%22+OR+%22setondrop%22+OR+%22scopeguard%22+OR+%22struct+ondrop%22%29+language%3ARust&type=code&repo=&langOverride=&start_value=1&l=Rust&p=3)). That’s a hit rate of 16%, which to me seems too low to change the default. Especially since that 16% of cases is already supported today by writing:

```rust
let _guard = DropGuard::new((), |_| ..);
```

Perhaps there is a way to introduce a convenience function that doesn’t change the default? For example something like `DropGuard::with_default` that defaults to passing `()`. But that’s something which could always be considered later.

body:
guess the error message already says to coerce the object to a pointer, but we could also show a suggested code snippet. I think that would also let rust-analyzer make the change automatically. Given an error message like:

```
error: `black_box` on zero-sized callable `fn(u32, u32) -> u32 {add}` has no effect on call opacity
  --> $DIR/lint-black-box-zst-call.rs:10:18
   |
LL |     let add_bb = black_box(add);
   |                  ^^^^^^^^^^---^
   |                            |
   |                            zero-sized callable passed here
   |
   = note: zero-sized callable values have no runtime representation, so the call still targets the original function directly
   = help: coerce to a function pointer and call `black_box` on that pointer instead
   ```
   
   The suggestion would be something like:

```
    let add_ptr: fn(u32, u32) -> u32 = add;
    let add_bb = black_box(add_ptr);
```

We have some other errors and warnings that make suggestions like this, so I'd try to look at what they do and see if you can reuse that infrastructure.
author: eholk
traviscross

body: I don't think the terminology "zero-sized callable" appears anywhere else. Should we be more specific and say "function item" or "closure" here?
author: Darksonn

body:
There is another footgun potential here. For the specific case of closures, using `black_box` is probably wrong even if the closure's hidden type is has non-zero size.

After all, such use of `black_box` will apply the black box effect to the values of captured variables, but like here, it has no effect on call opacity.

Yes, I understand what you are saying. I looked into it and it doesn't look like `help2man` supports two separate help options. author: arielb1

body:
Yeah, I don't mind erasing the nuance here but I added it to point out where this form of misuse will have a significant impact.

If you have a codebase that is a large workspace of unpublished crates and your codebase is the only one that ever interacts with and reports your errors if you duplicate this information in the chain but never observe it because you only ever call provide on the outermost error in the chain and don't care about identifying exactly which error it came from then its like a tree falling in the forest with nobody around to hear it. If in the future you change your mind and want to associate context with specific errors in the chain of your reports and your implementation becomes a problem you as the author can trivially fix it because you control the definition and all invocations of your errors.
