author: folkertdev

body:
Yeah, we should allow "normal Rust" programs that don't need cursed C interop to compile for wasm targets.

Writing a simple wasm interpreter is a bit of work but simply not very hard, and "everyone knows" Rust works well on wasm, so since wasm64 at its base is not that much more than wasm32, I'd want anyone working on making the target more functional to be able to do that much.

And to encounter a loud "you should fix this" error if they run into some edge case.
author: workingjubilee

body:
> > The target is being used in production, so it definitely is in working condition
> 
> Huh, what is used to execute wasm64 binaries then?

Usually anything that can execute Wasm32, e.g. Chrome, Firefox, Node.js.
See the [official Wasm feature status table](https://webassembly.org/features).
author: daxpedda

body:
> And to encounter a loud "you should fix this" error if they run into some edge case.

Right. I've added the `bug!` call now, so we should maybe give the target maintainers a couple more days but then this should be ready.

> Usually anything that can execute Wasm32

That feature table only mentions memory64, but I thought that was something different from a full wasm64 target.
author: folkertdev

body:
> > Usually anything that can execute Wasm32
> 
> That feature table only mentions memory64, but I thought that was something different from a full wasm64 target.

From a Wasm engines perspective basically the only difference between Wasm32 and Wasm64 is that the memory and tables use 64-bit address types, which requires the Memory64 proposal. That's it; nothing more is required.
author: daxpedda

body:
> another round of wasm ABI grief

heh thanks for the consideration! I'd agree with y'all here that `bug!` seems the way to go.

> Huh, what is used to execute wasm64 binaries then?

I'd echo what @daxpedda said in that wasm64 is almost trivial to support in an engine, but 90% of the complications are in the guest toolchain itself. For example there's no such thing as `wasm64-wasip{1,2,3,..}` right now (although there is desire for such a target). The wasm64 target in Rust is also tier 3, so effectively the only "big" users of wasm64 that I know of are emscripten based which has done more work to ship it toolchain-side.

body:
I tried `max_atomic_width: Some(32)` and even with a relaxed load/store I got:

```text
rust-lld: error: undefined symbol: __sync_val_compare_and_swap_4
```

So for the same reason as ARMv4T and ARMv5TE I propose just switching atomics off here, and letting portable-atomic provide a work-around if it so chooses.
author: thejpster

body:
> I tried `max_atomic_width: Some(32)` and even with a relaxed load/store I got:
> 
> ```
> rust-lld: error: undefined symbol: __sync_val_compare_and_swap_4
> ```
>
> So for the same reason as ARMv4T and ARMv5TE I propose just switching atomics off here, and letting portable-atomic provide a work-around if it so chooses.

Thanks for confirming. LLVM just generates `__sync_*` builtins instead of raising errors (like they do for __yield), so I believe the situation is simpler.

I believe more appropriate approach here is to implement the `__sync_*` builtins in compiler-builtins with `instruction_set(arm::a32)` and inline assembly.
author: taiki-e

body:
> I believe more appropriate approach here is to implement the `__sync_*` builtins in compiler-builtins with `instruction_set(arm::a32)` and inline assembly.

Opened https://github.com/rust-lang/compiler-builtins/pull/1050 which implements this approach. 

If you and the compiler-builtins maintainer are okay with that approach, we can set the max-atomic-width to 32 and atomic-cas to true for thumbv6-none-eabi after the new compiler-builtins version containing that change is released.
author: taiki-e

body:
These targets look good to me. Regarding the naming: `armv6-*` seems fine, we have some `arm-*` targets that are implicitly v6 but we also already have some `armv6-*` targets that are explicitly v6. rust-lang/compiler-builtins#1050 seems like the right approach to me, I'll leave it to @taiki-e to advise on what the right order to merge these is for that.

body: I think that this is probably fine. It can't cause the answer to "does Foo implement Other<T>" to depend on lifetimes, since satisfying the impl block's where bounds is a necessary and sufficient condition for implementing the subtrait. If the fully generic where bounds didn't imply implementing the supertrait already, then we would have already errored in the impl block.

body:
After some back and forth with @theemathas I have come to the conclusion that we do not need to elaborate bounds. The fact that we restrict *all* impls that are part of figuring out a `Some` output of `try_as_dyn` means that we allow this fully generic impl, but then reject

```rust
impl Trait for &'static () {}
```

And you can't cheat and do sth weird like

```rust
impl<'a> Trait for &'a () {}
```

because of the `trait Trait: 'static {}`.
author: oli-obk

body:
min spec almost always compares two impls, which we could probably somewhat re-use (the second impl can just be the fake one by the trait which defines its default methods)

Also all the spec logic just errors, while want to silently return `None` (or `Err` in the future if we want).

So yea, we can share logic, but it needs a larger refactoring of the spec logic.

body:
Very true, let's go with the following example, then:

```rs
impl<'a, 'b> Trait<'b> for &'a str
where
    Option<&'a str>: IntoIterator<Item = &'b str>,
{}
```

which amounts to having done:

```rs
impl<'a> Trait<'a> for &'a str {}
```

which brings us back to "no repetitions in header" (`&'? str: Trait<'static>` discriminates `'?` against `'static`).

---

````suggestion
Associated-type equality bounds can very much amount to lifetime-infected parameter equality constraints, which are problematic as per the "at most one mention of each lifetime-infected parameter in header" rule.
To illustrate, with the following definitions, `&'? str: Trait<'static>` discriminates `'?` against `'static`:

```rust
impl<'a, 'b> Trait<'b> for &'a str
where
    //     &'a str = &'b str,
    Option<&'a str>: IntoIterator<Item = &'b str>,
{}

impl<'a> Trait<'a> for &'a str {}
```
````
author: danielhenrymantilla

Should we also handle cases like `&raw const STATIC[idx]` or `&raw mut STATIC[start..end]`?

Coming back to the original issue: since `std::ops::Index::index` takes `&Self` as its receiver, the *technically* correct form would be `unsafe { &*&raw const STATIC }[idx]`. That said, `unsafe { &*&raw const STATIC[idx] }` might already be sufficient to convey the user’s intent—that they are explicitly acknowledging and accepting the potential UB. 🤔

body:
The allowed targets list is not correct.
According to the spec, the allowed targets are:

> The instruction_set attribute may only be applied to functions with [bodies](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/items/functions.html#r-items.fn.body) — [closures](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/expressions/closure-expr.html#r-expr.closure), [async blocks](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/expressions/block-expr.html#r-expr.block.async), [free functions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/items/functions.html#r-items.fn), [associated functions](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/items/associated-items.html#r-items.associated.fn) in an [inherent impl](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/items/implementations.html#r-items.impl.inherent) or [trait impl](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/items/implementations.html#r-items.impl.trait), and associated functions in a [trait definition](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/items/traits.html#r-items.traits) when those functions have a [default definition](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/items/traits.html#r-items.traits.associated-item-decls) .

(https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/attributes/codegen.html#the-instruction_set-attribute)
author: JonathanBrouwer

body:
Bitshits indeed 😄

I don't know anything for a fact, and it would be REALLY hard to deterministically assert that ALL JS interpreters can figure it out, but... I would be surprised if a) V8 and its cohorts can't figure it out and; b) it actually makes a difference in performance (an interpreted language has so much more overhead that one arithmetic op probably can't be that significant)

Personally, I think that the guiding principle here should be readability over performance. What makes more logical sense - a bitshi(f)t op, or multiplication?

This is not the way to go about it. This should remain a panic since the callers should handle this pattern in a special way.

I'm pretty sure that this will lead to `fn(: Ty)` getting printed instead of `fn(Ty)`. To fix this, callers should only print the pattern & the colon if the pattern isn't missing.

On my phone but I'm curious why we're only seeing this ICE now. This should be super simple to trigger via `rustc -Zunpretty=normal` or `rustc -Zunpretty=expanded`. Need to check.

**Edit**: Curiously, rustc correctly prints such signatures under both these modes, whether macro-generated or not, so I'm not sure yet how rustdoc is able to make ast-pretty reach this case.
author: fmease

body: Ah okay, so rustdoc manually calls `pat_to_string` inside `macro_expansion`. We should probably add a *Panics* section to `pat_to_string` instructing users to never print `Missing` since they should handle it on their own. And then we need to update rustdoc to do exactly that.

body: Could've made `Missing` an intra-doc link to ensure that we update the docs if it were to get removed or renamed (hypothetically). Doesn't really matter though.

body:
> Maybe this is not needed, as `PatKind::Missing` only appears in types. ccc3e425e8e2a4156c08e9c1f0239318390c59fb should handle the two issues at once.

No, it can also appear in trait methods in the 2015 edition:

```rs
trait Trait { fn method(Option<i32>); }
```

However, since we explicitly handle items in `visit_items` (like we handle types now, too, in `visit_ty`), that didn't lead to an ICE in rustdoc.

I think it would be better for this to tell LLVM that the output has self.size_hint().0 elements -- if we write that directly, it should mean that LLVM can also better make use of that length for further optimization in the error path. body: Given the logic above this might need to be more generic, calling it a branch instead of a remote branch, or reference the commit sha...
author: estebank

body:
Both calls to `git branch --points-at` (locals only and remotes) will incur a linear cost on the number of branches that exist in the repo. This can be a problem as there are some projects with *lots* of branches. We have the time cost of filtering them *and* the potential of lots branches pointing to the same tip, which is handled somewhat already.

The decision on whether the risk is acceptable, or we should not even try, is one I am flip-flopping and keep arguing with myself.

I reached out to the [git mailing list to see if they could add the equivalent of ` branch-name` so that this is unnecessary](https://lore.kernel.org/git/CAHnEOG2o784dk+OpkGt-1qjRJb34=sFMJvh-JRJ3v+GNBxFywQ@mail.gmail.com/T/#u).

body:
If we fail target checking, it does not remove the attribute, so we still reach this code.
The idea behind this is that we want to emit as many errors as possible, though in this case it might make sense not to.
Given this, I'd replace the comment with
```
Ignore this attribute if it's on an incorrect target, will already have error-ed in attribute target checking.
```
Furthermore, in this branch you can emit a delayed bug, to make sure we already errored:
```
self.dcx().delayed_bug(...)
```

body: Pattern types are a bit confusing. I believe the current behaviour (before this PR) is to *not* treat them as int-or-char, and instead use special logic in the equality test to transmute the scrutinee value from pattern type to underlying type.
author: Zalathar

body:
> Currently, range tests can only be initiated by range patterns.

True, but they can still interact with `TestableCase::Constant` so I find the `Eq` name confusing. Basically I'd like to see the `(TestKind::Range(range), &TestableCase::Constant { value, kind: _ })` line above have a sensible `kind` to put there. I think it's just `Bool | IntOrChar | Float`?
author: Nadrieril

body:
> I believe the current behaviour (before this PR) is to _not_ treat them as int-or-char, and instead use special logic in the equality test to transmute the scrutinee value from pattern type to underlying type.

Huh, weird. Does that mean they don't get a `SwitchInt`, and don't support range patterns?
author: Nadrieril

body:
I agree that the `Eq` kind is a bit confusing, but that's mostly a consequence of this PR trying to mostly reflect the existing behaviour, which is already confusing.

E.g. The reason the Range/Constant case uses `kind: _` is because that *is* the pre-existing behaviour.

I have a local follow-up draft that tries to split `Eq` into “string” and ”scalar”, where the scalar case covers floats, raw pointers (derived from integers), and pattern types (with their special extra transmute step). But that involves some further changes to split apart the `TestKind::Eq` lowering into two separate code paths.
author: Zalathar

body:
> Huh, weird. Does that mean they don't get a `SwitchInt`, and don't support range patterns?

Yeah, I believe they currently don't get a `SwitchInt`, and probably don't support range patterns.

From what I can tell, matching for pattern-types is still in a very underbaked state. It works well enough to pass tests, but hasn't yet been nicely integrated into the rest of the match-lowering design.
author: Zalathar

body:
I'd be happy to merge if you add `PatConstKind::Float` and change this line:
```suggestion
            (TestKind::Range(range), &TestableCase::Constant { value, kind: Bool | IntOrChar | Float }) => {
```
Otherwise I feel like this is slightly adding confusion on top of the existing one.

body:
Why did you change this line?  It's not "cleanup" and it's also now wrong, because the contract is on `self`, not `self.v`.

(It makes it seem like you did this with AI without actually looking at the diff.)
author: scottmcm

body:
No AI was involved. 

I'm not sure I agree that this is wrong (or that it would be correct with `self` instead of `self.v`). The call to `split_at_mut` only puts a requirement on `self.v`; if `self.v.len()` is correct, then any `split_at_mut` call is safe. This could even become `split_at_mut_unchecked` since it is always in bounds due to `let end = self.v.len() - end;`

I do think the safety comment for the second call to `split_at_mut` is suboptimal, since the receiver is not `self.v`, but `rest`, but this problem is pre-existing.

body:
I recommend making a local project for this and look at the output of `cargo expand` to understand the errors here. 

anyways
```rust
// FIXME(derive-where#136): Need to use separate `derive_where` for
// `Copy` and `Ord` to prevent the emitted `Clone` and `PartialOrd`
// impls from incorrectly relying on `T: Copy` and `T: Ord`.
#[derive_where(Ord; I: Interner, T: Ord)]
#[derive_where(Copy; I: Interner, T: Copy)]
#[derive_where(Clone, PartialOrd, PartialEq, Eq, Hash; I: Interner, T)]
```
please use this in all relevant parts of the codebase

Unfortunately, checking function's bounds against the expected input tys regresses `tests/ui/higher-ranked/trait-bounds/hrtb-doesnt-borrow-self-2.rs` in the old solver.

https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/99ff3fbb86658b427f5dd7daaae8db5626a63c26/tests/ui/higher-ranked/trait-bounds/hrtb-doesnt-borrow-self-2.rs#L19-L70

Evaluating the bounds `for<'b> &'b mut Self: Stream` on method `StreamExt::mapx` causes overflow while trying to consider the candidate `&'_ mut Map<Map<Map<...>, _>, _>, _>` and this ends up with a fatal error 😢

body:
> My understanding is that if I don't add the symbols __rustc_proc_macro_decls_* and rust_metadata_downstream_cdylib_* to the local list, then they should be global (which is what you call the allowlist)?

A proc-macro should only export those symbols, even with cstyle-export-rules enabled. So for a proc macro you should generate roughly `{ global: __rustc_proc_macro_decls_*; rust_metadata_downstream_cdylib_*; local: * }` just like before this PR. A proc-macro also doesn't export `#[no_mangle]` symbols either.

> Another problem is that I tried printing out the symbols I added to the local list, but I didn't find the leaked Rust ABI symbols from my test symbol table

No idea what the issue is there.

body:
This moves code A down into impl block B, but the diff shows B being moved up past A.

So this actually moves `into_raw` and `into_non_null` (expand below this seeming addition) into an impl block that is generic over the allocator, such that they can accept Boxes that use an allocator different from the Global one. This is needed for the changes in linked_list.rs to replace `Box::leak` (which is also generic over allocator).

This probably needs some libs discussion/approval.

author: llogiq

body: Among other things it uses `#[may_dangle]` for arenas, enables the nightly feature of hashbrown, uses rustc_macros (which needs `feature(proc_macro_diagnostics, proc_macro_tracked_env)`), rustc_serialize (which needs specialization) and it uses a whole bunch more unstable features: https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/24ac030a796b9e39bc9d07b6463e633df48e485e/compiler/rustc_data_structures/src/lib.rs#L15-L36
author: bjorn3

body: I see. So the best we can do for now is to change this on nightly?
author: llogiq

body: Using `rustc_hash::FxBuildHasher` would work on stable. Centralizing all `rustc_hash` imports to `rustc_data_structures::fx` can only be done on nightly while rustc_data_structures needs nightly.

body:
You have exhibited a number of hallmarks of AI-driven coding:
1. deleting or changing things out of scope of the PR... these are common when using mechanical assists of any kind, not just LLMs
2. many *seemingly* purely-stylistic changes with no evidence or argument for them in their commit descriptions, the substance of the commit, or in the PR description, that may have semantic changes (especially in the future!)
3. a bulleted list in your PR description that includes an item that is *still* not actually diffed by the PR, even after I asked for you to do the actual cleanup in the actual tests
4. confidently asserting something with no textual referent that I am equally confident, based on my experience, is simply wrong... this is the most unique to someone being fed information from LLMs, especially for a new contributor

The way you present yourself otherwise seems organic, so I suspect you are not using it much. I may even be wrong and merely blaming machines for human error... I'm a bullet-list-enjoyer myself, and have sometimes failed to doublecheck them. Accordingly, I am going to let you off with a personal warning: maintain your focus in your contributions and keep them narrowly scoped. I know this can be hard... I require significant chemical assistance to do this... but this is your first PR here, and if you want to come back here you are going to need to do a lot better in your focus on average before people will start to trust more sweeping changes from you.

Redo this PR from the start with the *minimal*, precise changeset necessary to make the Ordering impl reflect how it is written in core and be removed from the tests it appears in. It need not be line-for-line exact, but yes, a "minor" thing like `Less = -1` matters. Do this while making sure every commit explains itself in its title, comment, and content, or refers appropriately to the source justifying the change, and I will accept it. If I see more out-of-scope, unexplained and unrelated changes, I will close it without further requests for changes. Similarly, if I find it is incomplete because it does not diff something it claims to be cleaning up... even if it does not explicitly list it... I will also close it.

I have already written enough that on reflection this should probably already be closed under the policy of https://github.com/rust-lang/compiler-team/issues/893 but I suppose I am feeling exceptionally charitable purely out of the spirit of the holidays. Maybe because I would also feel bad if this was just you being ADHD like I am.

body:
I've changed this so that people can optionally return an `ExitCode` when using `catch_with_exit_code` rather than having to use `process::exit`.

Note that the `Termination` trait is implemented for `()` so this shouldn't make a difference to existing code unless they opt-in to returning `ExitCode` (or anything else that implements the trait).
author: ChrisDenton

body: Question: not blocking, but was there some larger context for this change? The change itself makes sense to me, just curious, cuz it *feels* like there's some context elsewhere.
author: jieyouxu

body: When I originally looked into it there wasn't a concrete motivation other than the fact we now have `Termination` and it's the correct thing to do. But  one motivating example that convinced me it was worthwhile was rustdoc not dropping the temp dir if `process::exit` is called (and thus not doing cleanup). See https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/139899. While that specific situation has been worked around it did make me think a more general solution was warranted.

body:
In theory we ought to make the same change to the match arms for handling arays/slices/tuples. They're currently doing the same `ty::Const::new_value(ct.to_value())` thing which they shouldn't be. I don't think it's possible to write a test case where this matters yet though (we would need mgca to have support for `hir::ConstArgKind::Array/Tuple` which it doesn't yet).

I feel like a more involved refactor could be nice. Specifically:
- Stop handling `Array`/`Slice`/`Tuple` in `destructure_const`
- Have all callers of `destructure_const` handle array/slice/tuples themselves by using the `branches` of the `ValTree`.
- Rename `destructure_const` and `ty::DestructuredConst` to `destructure_adt_const` and `ty::DestructuredAdtConst`

Would you be interested in doing that? Can either do so in this PR or a future PR if you like. I'm happy to just merge this PR without the proposed refactoring if you prefer.

body:
I had to refresh my memory of the macro... the docs seem a bit outdated; it says "The runtime function is always marked as `#[inline]`" but actually both functions are marked as inline by default and `noinline` can be used to suppress that.

This here is the only user of `#[noinline]` so if we don't want that any more, maybe we should just remove it?

Anyway for the comment, the one this comment is attached to indeed does not seem useful. The one a few lines down is confusing. It's unclear what "it" refers to, and also there's no panic_fmt visible here, so... what is t talking about?^^
author: RalfJung

body:
> It's unclear what "it" refers to

I was intending the two comments to read as one sentence but of course they aren't actually one so it doesn't work.

When `assert!` fails, it calls a panic entrypoint,, which like all the panic entrypoint functions is `#[cold]`. Since the failure branch leads directly to a block which is terminated by a call to a `#[cold]` function, codegen produces a branch hint. This "SwitchInt arm leads to #[cold]" logic is actually how `intrinsics::likely` is implemented.

But in `const_panic!` we have this wrapper function around the runtime failure arm to make `const_eval_select` work. Adding `#[inline]` to the wrapper function means that the wrapper gets inlined away in MIR and the cold-ness of what it wraps is now visible to codegen. Which brings back the branch hint information that we lose by just removing `intrinsics::likely`.
author: saethlin

body: Just FYI, putting codegen tests for multiple similar functions in the same file has a hidden hazard that I discovered in https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/pull/148849. You need the right `CHECK-DAG`s in place or the test for a function which is textually earlier can skip past the end of the function and match IR in another function.
author: saethlin

body:
Oh, that is gnarly... specifically it matches IR in a function that is otherwise ignored in the test, right? It can't match one of the functions we check with CHECK-LABEL since then it would later fail to find that label.

This seems worth at least tracking in a separate issue, and eventually we should systematically do that for all codegen tests.

body:
`*T` is not how Rust's pointer types are written, nor C's and that's not what the user wrote in https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/76173. I doubt anyone is really writing `<*T>` by accident, even beginners or people sufficient in C -- and even if they were, the diagnostic is ~clear enough.

We actually have the opposite problem: It seems we're already trying to parse a type in that position for recovery since we suggest adding `mut` / `const` after the `*` for `fn f<*T>` on main which is subpar tbh (ideally we'd turn off that subsequent recovery)? Not sure what's going on there, haven't checked.

In any case, just checking for `*` to "work towards" addressing #76173 is not the way to go about it imo since that's way too specific. We should just try to parse any type (but see paragraph above) and provide a more general diagnostic. The likelihood of sb. writing `fn f<*const T>() {}` like the user in #76173 is astronomically low, it's basically a one-off I'd bet.

Therefore generalizing it *might* have some practical merit (catching people write `fn f<[T]>()`, idk) but I'm actually unconvinced that we should accommodate #76173 at all. Personally speaking I'd just close that issue as "not planned" thinking about it.

*[View changes since this review](https://triagebot.infra.rust-lang.org/gh-changes-since/rust-lang/rust/150401/8da80d36a3b8fe4c0cc737b1e9494ce33a091f01..c36145fe8d6930700f98c73721b5610eed88b3a1)*

author: workingjubilee

body:
Yay test... but why doesn't it have any FileCheck comments to verify the LLVMIR for this looks a particular way? Is it because we are just hoping for LLVM to scream because it will fail isel if the debuginfo generation turns wrong? I feel like it should at least contain a basic "existence check" for *something*, here, as I don't feel super-confident that this test would always have failed before this PR, because in-principle LLVM doesn't have to reach actual machine instruction generation in order to emit LLVMIR, and this test suite only evaluates LLVMIR generation.

The problem as I understand it is about LLVM's failure during machine instruction selection. Even if it happens to *also* always perform it for this test suite, it doesn't actually relate, and it should be in "tests/assembly-llvm" if we aren't actually testing the shape of LLVMIR but want to assert something about the shape of the assembly.

If it shouldn't actually have any FileCheck comments at all, though, it should be in "tests/ui" and keep the existing `//@ build-pass` annotation, because that's where we put "the build works at all" tests.
author: workingjubilee

body: I believe this is the line that enforces that LLVM's ISel happens. Note that its sister test, simple.rs, does not have this line. Almost nothing in the codegen-llvm test suite has the `//@ build-pass` annotation, and we shouldn't increase the exception count beyond 1 without reason.

body:
Hmm. Yeah, I think the names should remain the same as they are now because those are the names most familiar to C programmers, and I think that (soon-to-be-former, hopefully) C programmers poking at the source will want to see them instead of some offbeat `va_list_clone` call.

I do understand the implication that it is a "false friend", because someone might assume it is LLVM's impl. However, here we do document it is not, and people who learn how Rust's intrinsics work will know that it is at most a delegation to LLVM's intrinsics (and how to find what they do). They will first see the Rust source, anyways!

And in a sense, I think they _aren't_ false friends: they are faithful implementations of `va_copy` and `va_end`... in Rust, a language which isn't C, but nonetheless implements some C semantics by assuming the C implementation can be made-compatible with Rust semantics.

We could name them `rust_va_copy` and `rust_va_end` if you really want to discourage people from assuming they are merely LLVM calls.  I also do appreciate that you have spent a while with your head "in LLVM" and you have to make such a distinction between `@llvm.va_copy` and our code anyways. I think LLVM makes the right choice here... even if the intrinsic, in practice, isn't actually what clang uses 99.9% of the time (in terms of compiler invocations, anyways? maybe it's used for more targets in absolute count than I think it is, but then again LLVM doesn't have such a highly reified notion of target tuples like we do...).

body:
This is actually a very awkward thing which I'm not fully sure how to solve nicely.

Here is how it goes:
1. We want to special case `ptr::drop_in_place` such that its argument is treated as `&mut T` instead of `*mut T`
2. To do that `arg_of` closure here checks for `is_drop_in_place && arg_idx == Some(0)`
3. ...however when we call `arg_attrs_for_rust_scalar` we do this in an `ArgAbi::new`, which desugars `*mut dyn Trait` into two arguments -- the data pointer and the vtable pointer
4. ...so `arg_attrs_for_rust_scalar` is called twice with `drop_target_pointee` being `Some(_)`.
5. I rewrote the code so that it does `drop_target_pointee_info.or_else(|| layout.pointee_info_at(&cx, offset))` instead of doing what the old code was doing (only checking `drop_targer_pointee` if `pointee.safe` is none, which is only true for the non-vtable pointer)
6. The `filter` here makes sure we only call `arg_attrs_for_rust_scalar` with `drop_target_pointee` being `Some(_)` once, for the data part

I didn't like the old version with conditionally overwriting `pointee` in the `arg_attrs_for_rust_scalar`, IMO it was confusing. The current version is also weird, so if you have ideas on how to improve this I can try them ^^'
author: WaffleLapkin

body:
They way I expect `MaybeDangling` to work in SB/TB (and the way you implemented it in #150446), it would be legal to transmute an `&mut T` to `MaybeDangling<&T>`, pass that around, transmute it back, and write to it.

`MaybeDangling` is a bad name, we just haven't found a better one. It really means "ignore all memory/aliasing-related requirements for references and `Box` inside of this".

author: juntyr

body: this function takes a pointer in the generic address space, so why this condition?
author: juntyr

body:
Pointers in Rust are _mostly_ in the generic address space, but there are exceptions.
For example with the **avr-none** target, `*const u32` would be in the generic address space (so, no problem to convert),
but `*const fn()` is in address space 1 (due to the `P1` in the data layout), therefore converting it to the generic address space involves an address space cast.

If we say this must always be possible (well, one can write `*const fn() as *const u32` in safe Rust and it results in an addrspacecast), I can remove this safety comment.
author: Flakebi

body: Thanks for the explanation! Maybe the comment could be clarified to "If the target's address space for this pointer type (e.g. data pointer vs function pointer) matches the generic address space ..."
author: juntyr

body: This seems like a very fragile hack -- having fields that then get removed half-way through compilation. There's a huge risk of confusing some part of the compiler that still looks at those fields. Not even `Box` does something this cursed, and that type is already quite cursed...
author: RalfJung

body:
I agree with your sentiment, I thought the same when I initially tried to get it working.
It is similar to `#[rustc_intrinsic]`s with a dummy/fallback body that gets replaced during compilation.
I would argue that while fallback intrinsic bodies have the risk of getting inlined and therefore silently mis-compile a program, the dummy struct body here **will always fail compilation if used** so it’s actually _safer_ than the intrinsics that rustc already uses.

Rustc so far only replaced function bodies, replacing a type body is something new, therefore I explicitly called this out in the PR description (in Somewhat Open Points).

> Note in particular that rustc needs to know the size and alignment of all its types without going through LLVM.

This works perfectly fine (see also the test I wrote in the PR), Rust knows the correct size and alignment because we replace the type layout of `AddrspacePtr` with a valid layout of a pointer to the specified address space.
Every access to the struct goes through the type layout, that’s where it fails compilation if some code tries to access the body.
author: Flakebi

body:
Re-reading my comment, I want to clarify it, so it doesn’t come across wrongly: I wanted to express that I think replacing the struct body as implemented here is not as bad as it looks at a first glance.
There are probably many ways to improve it, make it look better and more robust. But I didn’t find the better places to modify so far. So for everyone who reads this comment, if you know more about the Rust compiler than I do (well, that’s a given ;)) and you have concrete or not-so-concrete ideas for improvement, let me hear them!

author: RalfJung

body:
I wasn't sure where to put this. It's not an _optimization_, per se, but it is maintainable as a MirPass. This could also be pushed earlier into MIR building, but that seems worse to me.

There is `MirPass::is_required`, I thought making that return `true` was the right marker, the docs say:
> Returns true if this pass must be run (i.e. it is required for soundness). For passes which are strictly optimizations, this should return false. If this is false, #[optimize(none)] will disable the pass.
author: saethlin

body: Yes, this has a significant number of false positives _in general_, that's why it is internal. But across core+alloc there is only one.
author: saethlin

body: Might be good to mention that in the output and encourage people to `allow` it?
author: RalfJung

body: Looking at some of the other `is_required` passes, they don't seem to alter the spec quite as fundamentally as this one does. For instance, the passes that insert extra debug checks don't alter the spec at all, they just change what happens on UB. They are clearly not "required for soundness", so the comment seems off. This literally replaces function calls with aborts without any regards for the body of the function!

body:
So suppose we have a glob `g1` importing from module `m1`, and `m1` has another glob `g2` importing from module `m2`.
What this says is that when something in `m2` changes, we are re-fetching not only `g2`, but `g1` as well.
And `g1` will re-fetch updated bindings from `g2`.
(And of course there also may be more than 2 globs referring to each other in this chain.)
author: petrochenkov

body:
I don't think this is the case that applies to the diagnostic change in `tests/ui/imports/issue-55884-1.rs`.
(The diagnostic change itself looks right, not sure why it wasn't reported before, maybe due to the deduplication from `matches_previous_ambiguity_error`, I'll check.)
author: petrochenkov

body:
The `tests/ui/imports/issue-55884-1.rs` change is not due to the deduplication.

Previously `use m::S` recorded the outdated resolution of `m::S` that is not yet ambiguous, and `S {}` used it.
(Import validation later observed the updated ambiguous resolution though, and reported an error.)
Now the resolution is updated in place, so `S {}` sees it too, and reports the ambiguity error on use too.

body:
It looks great! When a test failed, it was not clear which specific configuration had an issue, so I implemented a bit of context propagation for run-make commands to make that clearer. It's [here](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/commit/7f38788ddc704949b5e92bfe31901b5109dbc664), in case you'd like to use it directly.

One thing I am slightly concerned about is the duration of the test and the RAM usage. It's not a problem per-se, but rather because we execute everything in a single test, and thus it won't participate fully in the normal parallel load-balancing of cargo/rustc/bootstrap/compiletest.

If we had revisions for run-make tests, we could just build the matrix array in the declarative header of the test, and then let compiletest generate the full matrix of the configurations. But I don't think that's working today, and I don't think it's worth blocking this PR on that.

I'd say let's try to land it and see if people start running into issues with the test.

We could thread it in some nicer way, but the `RUST_TEST_THREADS` environment variable is actually propagated from bootstrap to compiletest and then to the run-make test. And the value of this environment variable corresponds to `-j`. So you can try to use that as the thread count (if it exists).

It would be good to do this, because running everything in parallel seems to consume a lot of RAM.

body:
Generally the way it's done it's that bootstrap sets an env var, feeds it to compiletest (as Jakub describes), which gets picked up by a run-make-support wrapper, then the rmake.rs uses that wrapper to get the info.

(The indirection is so that if another rmake.rs wants to use this, they don't have to find out about the specific env var)

body: Your PR introduces a new `TAG_RELATIVE_OUTER_SPAN` tag here for encoding spans which are outside their parent span, this PR instead reuses the `TAG_RELATIVE_SPAN` tag. This can be done by encoding the positions in the span (which are u32s) subtracted from their parent, wrapping if necessary. This change seems to be most of the performance gained relative to your PR.

body: When hashing, your PR hashes an `isize` here, this PR instead hashes a `u32` by using a `wrapping_sub`. Haven't explored if this makes any perf difference

body: Does that mean this PR makes that code go from query cycle -> pass? I feel like it'd be nice to be more clear/explicit about which specific code is fixed by this PR. Afaict any code which doesn't actually need promotion to take place but had a const block there will now pass whereas was previously broken by #138499?
author: BoxyUwU

body:
The cycle there has the same shape, but I find it conceptually a bit different, since it arises from a cyclic dependency in user code: by definition, CTFE of `<()>::ASSOC` requires CTFE of `foo`, which requires CTFE of `<()>::ASSOC`. Even if we weren't trying to evaluate `<()>::ASSOC` during constant promotion, it and `foo` are both unusable. Since the cycle is unavoidable, I don't think we need to catch it in constant promotion.

The cycle for inline consts is due to how they're borrow-checked along with their typeck root. If borrow-checking the containing body requires evaluating the constant, this gives rise to a cycle, since evaluating the constant requires borrow-checking it, which again means borrow-checking the containing body. However, using the inline const is perfectly fine as long as we don't evaluate it until after borrow-checking.

I'll tack a note onto to the "other kinds of unevaluated's" comment to clarify why the inline const cycle in particular needs to be avoided.

body:
I see that this one came out `or`-then-`select`, but the one below is `select`-then-`or`.

I don't think we actually care about the ordering between them, since they're disjoint.  Thus please change them to use `CHECK-DAG` instead (see <https://llvm.org/docs/CommandGuide/FileCheck.html#the-check-dag-directive>) so that we don't cause spurious test failures when upgrading LLVM in case different versions happen to put them in different orders.

(And so that changes to the library code don't seemingly-randomly cause test failures, etc)

Here we have three different cases: 0, 1, 2..

When we have exhausted all possibilities in a range like this, it's nice to make that clearly visible instead of requiring close attention to the particulars of each sub-expression.

That could be achieved by something like
```rust
    let acyclic = match nodes.len() {
        0 => true,
        1 ==> !graph.successors.nodes([0]).any(x| x == nodes[0])),
        2.. => false,
    }
    if acyclic {
```

body:
Isn't this two comments, one on `user_map` and one on `used_map`? Or perhaps more concisely, something like

```suggestion
    // Be careful: subsets `used_map`, so unused items that use other items are vacant.
```
author: workingjubilee

body: Oh, or do you mean that here, an item that appears in `used_map` but uses nothing has `vec![]` as its entry data in `user_map`? Sure.
author: workingjubilee

body:
It is exactly the opposite: An item is vacant in `user_map` when it has no users, but an item that does not use another item has an empty `vec![]` as its entry data in `used_map`.

So I wanted to emphasize the difference between the two, because `used_map` really does provide a mapping for *every* mono item, while `user_map` does not. In my test the entry for `INT` is an empty vector in `used_map` but `BAR` is not present in `user_map` at all.

I was of the opinion that `user_map` and `used_map` are equivalent, because both comments stated that they contain a mapping for *every* mono item. I wanted to know the reason why using `user_map` lead to a panic while using `used_map` did not.

Your suggestion expresses this difference quite well in far fewer words, but I would say the restriction is not necessary. I'm in favor of:
```Rust
// Be careful: subsets `used_map`, so unused items are vacant.
```

body:
That's handled separately in the `variant_field` function. The reason there's a distinction is that paths to struct and union fields look the same as paths to enum variants and associated items (two segments), while paths to enum variant fields look different (three segments).

I agree that it's confusing that they're handled separately though. I think it would be simpler if we don't do this sort of path length-based separation.

body: It looks like this length-based logic means that re-exports of variants don't work properly (can't look up their fields). Although this is a pre-existing bug, we should definitely try to fix it in a future PR.

body:
This generally seems somewhat hacky, using `into_raw_parts` isn't great.

If you are creating a new allocation on each va_arg call anyway, why do you store the "list index" in the offset? That just poses the risk that someone does `add` to change where in the list we are. Shouldn't it also work to have just the remaining elements in the `va_list_map` and have the pointer always point at the "beginning" of the allocation? Then you can define a helper similar to `get_ptr_fn` that takes a pointer and returns the remaining va_arg list.
author: RalfJung

body: In the latest commit the `offset` is no longer used. That does not solve the issue though: provenance on the pointer is lost when it is written to and then read again from memory.
author: folkertdev

body:
I think I know why the provenance is being lost...

On x86, `VaListInner` is declared as starting with 8 integer bytes. We are storing pointer data there. When copying the `VaListInner`, that will drop the provenance.

What's less clear to me is where a `VaList` (and therefore its `inner` field) is being copied. But given that we expose this type to users I guess it is expected that they are allowed to copy it...

It seems like currently, all `VaListInner` types have a pointer field that we could use to store the pointer that the interpreter uses to represent the actual state. Not sure if that's a safe thing to bet on though...

*[View changes since this review](https://triagebot.infra.rust-lang.org/gh-changes-since/rust-lang/rust/150601/d29e4783dff30f9526eeba3929ebfe86c00c9dad..a564f243d051a823baf7afad9056d823710c74c6)*

These are too high-level to be in `memory.rs`. "intrinsics" seems like a better place? Or maybe a new va_args file.

`memory.rs` should have just very low-level operations, matching the rest of this file:
- given a `Vec<MPlaceTy<'tcx, M::Provenance>>`, construct a VaList allocation and return a pointer to that
- given a pointer, remove the corresponding VaList allocation, returning the corresponding `Vec<MPlaceTy<'tcx, M::Provenance>>`

Please keep the names for these consistent with the function and vtable methods. (The 2nd one is a kind of operation new, vtables and functions never get deallocated.)

Everything else should be in a different file.

body:
How does const eval/Miri treat this sort of UB. E.g. I believe that on many platforms you can just provide more arguments than the callee expects. They just disappear, but it's not UB as far as I know.

The other way around can work too. If a c-variadic callee has 3 fixed arguments, and a caller believes it's calling a non-variadic function with just those 3 arguments, that should work out in practice.

But I suspect miri is a bit stricter than that?
author: folkertdev

body:
We implement what is described at https://doc.rust-lang.org/nightly/std/primitive.fn.html#abi-compatibility (well, almost). In particular, the caller passing more arguments than the callee expects is absolutely UB (in both Rust and C).

Also, the number of fixed arguments must match. There are calling conventions where the same type is passed differently depending on whether it is fixed or variadic. "Works out in practice in some calling convention" is not good enough. So for now all of these kinds of mismatches are UB; we can always consider relaxing that later if someone shows up with a good use-case -- but the bar for that would be very high since such platform-specific UB is something we generally try to avoid.

Please add Miri tests for al these mismatches.

body:
The lowered const here would represent the value of the function item being called- not the const const representing the full tuple constructor call. I think what you need to do is:
- Update `lower_resolved_const_path` to lower CtorKind::Fn's to ZST `ConstKind::Value`s with a type of `FnDef`
- You'll want to do a similar thing for `lower_type_relative_const_Path`. Possibly also returning a `DefKind` and `DefId` from that function like `lower_type_relative_ty_path` does, though I'm not sure if that is necessary or not.

Then instead of looking for `DefKind::Variant` and `DefKind::Struct` resolutions (which can't occur from these `_const_path` functions) you should be checking for `DefKind::Ctor(_, CtorKind::Fn)`.
author: BoxyUwU

body:
It's important not to be using or changing `lower_resolved_ty_path` as that's logic for resolving stuff in the type namespace not the value namespace (where constructors are). With your current changes the logic for lowering type paths will now be able to resolve to constructors which is wrong.

The struct expression logic uses the ty path logic because we are actually resolving to a type (which struct or enum we're constructing). But for tuple constructor calls we're resolving to a value which is then called

body: personal drive-by comment: This signature has the same problem as `Arc::try_unwrap() -> Result` as compared to `Arc::into_inner() -> Option`: If two threads call `into_unique` at the same time without dealing with the error case (`into_unique().ok()`), they could both determine that they both are not the unique owners, and both (safely) discard the inner value. In order for `into_unique` to be used from multiple threads, all threads must always put the returned err-value back to the place where it came from, or it might get silently lost, because every thread thinks that some other thread now owns it...
author: lukaslueg

body: Rc isn't Send or Sync. Can you clarify how this method would be called from multiple threads?
author: cramertj

body: Rc isn't Send or Sync. Can you clarify how this method would be called from multiple threads?
author: cramertj

body: My bad, same comment, for the `Arc` implementation below, not `Rc`
author: lukaslueg

body:
Ah, okay. So you're not concerned that the implementation is incorrect, but you are concerned that two different callers could additionally assume (incorrectly) that they are not the last holder of an `Arc` after failing to call `into_unique`?

That is, this situation described in the docs of `Arc::try_unwrap`:

> It is strongly recommended to use [Arc::into_inner](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/sync/struct.Arc.html#method.into_inner) instead if you don’t keep the Arc in the [Err](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/result/enum.Result.html#variant.Err) case. Immediately dropping the [Err](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/result/enum.Result.html#variant.Err)-value, as the expression Arc::try_unwrap(this).ok() does, can cause the strong count to drop to zero and the inner value of the Arc to be dropped. For instance, if two threads execute such an expression in parallel, there is a race condition without the possibility of unsafety: The threads could first both check whether they own the last instance in Arc::try_unwrap, determine that they both do not, and then both discard and drop their instance in the call to [ok](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/result/enum.Result.html#method.ok). In this scenario, the value inside the Arc is safely destroyed by exactly one of the threads, but neither thread will ever be able to use the value.

This is a surprising comment to me. Even using the non-`Result`-based interface, the same race would occur if any holder of the `Arc` is dropping it by calling something other than `into_unique`. In order for this guarantee to work out (at least one caller gets the unique value) *all* instances of the `Arc` must be dropped using `into_unique`.

The `-> Option` version seems like a potentially useful interface to provide, but IMO it feels like much more of an edge-case than the more usual "try to mutate this `Arc` in-place as an optimization, allowing for the concurrent existence of non-upgradable `Weak` pointers". I would've called the `Option` version something like `unique_or_discard`, perhaps?

Perhaps we should align with the existing API by using `into_unique -> Option` and `try_into_unique -> Result`?
author: cramertj

body:
> Ah, okay. So you're not concerned that the implementation is incorrect, but you are concerned that two different callers could additionally assume (incorrectly) that they are not the last holder of an `Arc` after failing to call `into_unique`?

Yes, my comment was purely concerned with the potential TOCTOU-pitfall when using this API. There is an unavoidable gap that allows both threads to fail in `into_unique`, and then the inner value to be dropped entirely. Maybe a simple doc-example will do to point this out. After all, the `-> Option`-variant has exactly the same pitfall, which is unavoidable.
author: lukaslueg

body:
TBH, it's not really obvious to me that this is *better* for all these types.

Like here having the match emphasizes that this is unreachable better than just `*self` does, to me -- in MIR it's the difference between `_0 = copy *_1; Return` and `Unreachable`, for example.

And for phantomdata just being `_0 = PhantomData;` is more obviously address-uncaring than `_0 = copy *_1;` is.

Do you disagree, and think these are better?  Or were you just doing what clippy said?
author: scottmcm

body:
> TBH, it's not really obvious to me that this is _better_ for all these types.

That is fair (and I don't necessarily disagree), but perhaps the wrong way to view this change.

My goal is to enable a broader set of clippy lints for the compiler, specifically all of clippy::suspicious. This would have prevented bugs like https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/146940.

But in order to get there, all the positives need to be dealt with in some way. The possibilities for that are:
 - a simple code change (like this PR proposes)
 - a change to the lint category (like https://github.com/rust-lang/rust-clippy/issues/15736)
 - a change to the lint behavior (https://github.com/rust-lang/rust-clippy/issues/15200, https://github.com/rust-lang/rust-clippy/issues/15620)
 - an allow/expect annotation.

> Like here having the match emphasizes that this is unreachable better than just `*self` does, to me -- in MIR it's the difference between `_0 = copy *_1; Return` and `Unreachable`, for example.

My thinking was that the argument being Infallible is enough. If it is important to emphasize this, then perhaps `unreachable!()` would be the better implementation?

I'm intrigued about the MIR implications, but I must confess knowing almost nothing about MIR. If it is important, can you explain why?

> And for phantomdata just being `_0 = PhantomData;` is more obviously address-uncaring than `_0 = copy *_1;` is.

Again, I'm intrigued, but I'm not sure what you're getting at here, besides that you think this decreases the quality of the MIR.

> Do you disagree, and think these are better?

I think that these changes are largely neutral by themselves, but they are a small step towards an end goal which does have positive value. I'm happy to discuss alternative ways of dealing with these. 

> Or were you just doing what clippy said?

I hope I have addressed this.
author: hkBst

body:
> If it is important to emphasize this, then perhaps unreachable!() would be the better implementation?

No, because that's a `panic!()`.  Having it be `match *self {}` is the safe way of getting `unsafe { hint::unreachable_unchecked() }`.

---

I think my inclination is that I don't really care for the reference and phantomdata examples, so merging that would be fine.

For the uninhabited type, though, I think clippy should change to not suggest this clone implementation, because the rule of "for something uninhabited, all the method implementations should be `match self {}` or `match *self {}`" should win.  After all, that's what it is for `Debug`, for `PartialEq`, for ...

(Allowing would be fine for now since this PR probably shouldn't also change clippy.)
author: scottmcm

Alright, I've removed the change to Infallible for now, and will look into a fix from the clippy side for that.

The argument against is that people would like to use dirfd in security relevant contexts. You can build a less secure variant on top of dirfd but not really the other way around (well technically you can but defaulting to the less secure option has historically been a footgun).

But in any case, I think this is a question for libs-api.

body:
I don't know enough about this to have an opinion on that question.^^

But we can bikeshed the intrinsic as a whole a bit.
- Why do we need both `offload` and `offload_args`?
- Given that `offload` also has arguments, the name doesn't seem very descriptive. Maybe `offload_with_host_helper` or so? Just spitballing here as I have know idea how this all actually gets used. ;)
author: RalfJung

body:
`offload` is there to map all arguments to the GPU, and then launch a GPU kernel. It has a couple of extra args, to tell offload (ol) how many threads and blocks we want when launching it. That gpu kernel comes from a Rust function, which we compiled to the GPU.

`offload_args` also maps all the arguments to the GPU, and then launches a CPU function, which accepts pointers to GPU memory. That's relying on the called CPU function independently launching a GPU kernel. A lot of highly optimized libraries like cuBLAS, rocBLAS, cuDNN, etc. already do that. If you try to launch them with offload you will get a failure, because they are not meant to be launched from the GPU. If you launch them but pass CPU pointers, you will also get a failure, because all arrays are supposed to be in GPU memory. In the past NVIDIA used to partly support calling those functions from the GPU, but they dropped support. I could probably try to unify both offload intrinsics under one name, but with autodiff we also decided to split it into `autodiff_forward` and `autodiff_reverse` since they were behaving too differently, which otherwise made it confusing.

Under the hood, both create an `offload_begin_mapper` which copies data to the GPU, and an `offload_end_mapper`, which copies them back. The main intrinsic then also has a `tgt_target_kernel_launch`, which calls the GPU kernel. The `offload_args` intrinsic just calls the host function, but as you can see in the codegen test, we swap out the pointers before. This gives us the benefit of being able to optimize them both the same, so now both can use all the LLVM profiling/debugging/visualization tools (not yet tested, but should work) and both benefit from the LLVM opts that we started writing for them.

Happy to change the intrinsic name since we expect to have a pretty macro on top of it. My motivation for `offload_args` was, that this intrinsic only offloads arguments, whereas the full `offload` intrinsic offloads both the arguments and a function to the GPU.

Does that help?
author: ZuseZ4

body: It sounds a bit like offload_args is a lower-level primitive that could be used to implement offload... but it also seems like that's not actually how it works?
author: RalfJung

body:
They share a lot of the implementation, that's why this PR is so small, especially once the first 3 refactor commits from a different PR are merged. If you look at the commits, you'll see that I've added a boolean host flag. If it is set to true, we directly call the host function given to the intrinsic. The compilation pipeline in this case is also shorten, and a few of our optional args are set to None, since we don't need to generate all that much info for offload.

If the host boolean is set to false, we generate a `__tgt_target_kernel` (I love their naming style) and launch the function given to the intrinsic through that. This requires that we have previously compiled the function to the GPU, and embedded it's bitcode into our IR.

body:
`A` here is a **reference** to **invalid** memory, as the pointer behind `A` is only valid on the GPU but this code runs on the CPU (also the `cfg(not(target_os` should probably be `cfg(target_os` without the not).

It works out fine here because A is never accessed, but I think it violates Rust’s guarantees.
To be safe, `offload_args` needs to map the type as well. I.e.
```rust
offload_args::<_, (&T, &[T])>(|t: *const T, ts: (*const T, usize)| {}, (t, &[t]));
// etc. for other mapped types
```
author: Flakebi

body:
Yes you are right, that's what I was mentioning above to Ralf: https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/pull/150683/changes#r2706310846 That means some safe usages can be currently unsound till the follow-up pr lands, but it's a half implemented rustc intrinsic (which end-users aren't supposed to use anyway), so I'd argue it's ok. The alternative is to make this PR bigger, but it clearly already takes a while to review, so I'd rather have that separate.


Generally, I am very willing to put in significant effort to preserve noalias for the GPU kernels, which your type mapping I think does not do. If you watch the slides from my US LLVM dev talk, they show how I intend to keep it even for mut args like `&mut [f32]`.

Also for the vision, I want these two intrinsics to be the default, which should (thanks to our new llvm opts) almost always be fast enough. However, we'll also offer explicit host2device, device2host memtransfers for users. oli-obk had a nice idea for a thin wrapper type that represents that a type is now on the GPU. In that case we could prevent usages on the CPU, and enforce that people only pass data to the GPU kernel which is already on the GPU.

But I see this third explicit mode (well it's not really a fully orthogonal mode, more like an extension) a bit similar to unsafe, it should hopefully be rarely necessary.

body:
Replying here since it seems you posted that in the wrong thread

> Also for the vision, I want these two intrinsics to be the default, which should (thanks to our new llvm opts) almost always be fast enough. However, we'll also offer explicit host2device, device2host memtransfers for users. oli-obk had a nice idea for a thin wrapper type that represents that a type is now on the GPU. In that case we could prevent usages on the CPU, and enforce that people only pass data to the GPU kernel which is already on the GPU.
> 
> But I see this third explicit mode (well it's not really a fully orthogonal mode, more like an extension) a bit similar to unsafe, it should hopefully be rarely necessary.

This sounds like you are discussing the public, user-visible API. In this PR we are discussing the underlying language primitives (intrinsics) powering that API. Those don't have the same concerns. No user should ever directly call these intrinsics. The intrinsics should be designed for 
- ease of specification
- ease of implementation in backends

body:
Confusingly there are two `LitKind`s defined in `rustc_ast`:
- `rustc_ast::token::LitKind`
- `rustc_ast::ast::LitKind`

The former is directly from the tokenizer and doesn't contain all the information about the literal (i.e what the value is, or if there was suffix like `_u32`).

The latter is post-parsing and contains all of the information about the literal and has undergone some validation to check the literal is correct.

I think we should be using `rustc_ast::ast::LitKind` here.
author: BoxyUwU

body: You can convert the `rustc_ast::token::Lit` to a `rustc_ast::ast::LitKind` here by calling `self.lower_lit(literal, expr.span)` and that'll give you an `ast::LitKind` and a `Span` that you can use for `ConstArgKind::Literal`.
author: BoxyUwU

body:
In theory we should be calling `visitor.visit_lit` here. I don't think we actually *can* right now though as lots of implementers of `Visitor` expect that `visit_lit` is only called on literals inside of function bodies.

For now I think you can return the default result with `V::Result::output()` and leave a `// FIXME(mgca)` to handle const arg literals in HIR visitors at some point
author: BoxyUwU

body:
The way this function will need to work will be somewhat similar to `try_lower_anon_const_lit`, though different in some ways.

The `lit_to_const` query can be called to turn a literal into a `ty::Const` (which we do in `try_lower_anon_const_lit` so you can read that function to see how to call the query). It takes a `LitToConstInput` type which consists of:
- The `ast::LitKind` which you can get from your `ConstArgKind::Literal`
- Whether it was a negated literal or not
 - The type that we *expect* the literal to be of. In this case this is the type of the const generic parameter that this `ConstArgKind::Literal` is an argument to.

For now you can use `false` unconditionally for whether the literal is negated. Your changes don't support negated literals yet which is OK and I think it's probably best to start off not supporting them. We can handle this either in follow up PR by you or by someone else if you don't want to do that :)

If you look at `lower_const_arg` you'll see there's the `FeedConstTy` thing and then we do `let anon_const_type = tcx.type_of(...).instantiate(...);`. You'll want to bring that variable out of the `if` so that it's available to the whole function, then you can pass it into your `lower_const_arg_literal` and use it as the `ty` for `LitToConstInput`.

---

There are a few things missing from this explanation but I don't think we need to care about them for this PR. However, I'm going to list them anyway for future reference:
- `lit_to_const` doesn't handle error reporting
- `lit_to_const` right now is overly strict with regards to the input `Ty`  and will fail to lower to a `ConstKind::Value` in some cases when it's desirable to do so
- we shouldn't call lit_to_const if there are `_` (inference variables) in the `Ty`
- The way `FeedConstTy` is setup isn't really enough for the way we're using it in this function (i.e. it won't have a type when we're lowering array lengths)

body: I believe AVR has the same requirement and probably amdgcn too. Also why does nvptx not have `need_explicit_cpu` set? Would we ever need to set `need_explicit_cpu` without `cpu_is_target_modifier` and vice versa?
author: bjorn3

body:
I wouldn't say that both flags have the same semantics. To me `need_explicit_cpu` should be set, when no good default exists. `cpu_is_target_modifier`, on the other hand, should be set when using different `-Ctarget-cpu` values can produce artifacts that are not soundly composable when linked together.

I would agree that both flags should be set for nvptx.

I don't know enough about AVR and amdgcn to answer what is the case for them.
But at least there are tests for both targets which indicate it might not be sound to link objects with different target-cpus: [AVR](https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/blob/597ffbe09d5f774f861ee55e50022bf84d7f98e2/lld/test/ELF/avr-flags.s), [amdgcn ](https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/blob/597ffbe09d5f774f861ee55e50022bf84d7f98e2/lld/test/ELF/amdgpu-elf-flags-err.s). So it might indeed be the case for both targets `cpu_is_target_modifier` should be set.
Given that, at the moment there wouldn't be a target in rustc with different values for `need_explicit_cpu` and `cpu_is_target_modifier`.
author: kulst

body: Seems a bit hard to imagine a target where there is no default but also all CPU values are compatible with each other.

body:
`"native"` is currently handled separately in each codegen backend. Performing the check there would result in duplication.

Instead, I moved the _parsing_ into its own function: `rustc_session::config::is_native_cpu`. It is called by all codegen backends and also by the new check.
The check itself now lives in `rustc_session::session::validate_commandline_args_with_session_available`, which seems more appropriate.

Is that in line with what you had in mind?

Having flags that change ABI is a problem because everything quietly breaks if you don't rebuild the world (among other problems); I don't think we want to add this to any more targets, we're trying to rip it out. See context at https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/116344, cc @RalfJung.

The thing to do is to submit an MCP to create a new separate `-softfloat` target, like we have with `aarch64-unknown-none-softfloat`, `loongarch64-unknown-none-softfloat`, `x86_64-unknown-none` (softfloat is implied there), and others.

Also cc @uweigand @cuviper target maintainers

author: tgross35

body:
there is only **one** valid abi on s390x. This ABI uses `hard-float`. 

The idea is that `soft-float`remains `incompatible` unless the user explicitly requests to use the `soft-float` abi(e.g.[^1]). This will not *change* the abi, as there is no other ABI specified. And to my knowledge there wont be a soft-float abi specified that really could be used by llvm. 

It is used as a gate, so if you want to  use soft-float you also have to explicitly change the ABI. And make it clear that this code is incompatible with the normal code.


[^1]: https://github.com/torvalds/linux/compare/master...fneddy:linux:add_rustc_s390x#diff-8b2b79dc9fe8130845984440e8df685c928623dfe1ebd12fe937efe4ab352aa9R267
author: fneddy

body:
> This will not _change_ the abi, as there is no other ABI specified.

What exactly do you mean by this - floats still get passed in vregs even with `soft-float`? What does the flag do then?
author: tgross35

body:
There might only be one official ABI, but the soft-float ABI is real and we're not going to pretend that it isn't. The soft-float ABI is whatever LLVM does to pass float args across function barriers when the `soft-float` target feature is on. So there might be no specification for such an ABI, but that doesn't stop LLVM from using it.

That's what happened on x86, anyway. I suspect it is similar on s390x.
author: RalfJung

body:
> so if you want to use soft-float you also have to explicitly change the ABI.

Exactly. But `self.abi` does not change the ABI, it just tells `cfg` what the ABI is. `llvm_abiname`, `llvm_floatabi`, and `rustc_abi` change the ABI.

(Well, that's not entirely true. `self.abi` actually is used in some places in the ABI adjustment logic. It's all a bit messy unfortunately...)
author: RalfJung

body:
@fneddy I assume that by saying there is only "one" "valid" ABI, you may be trying to say something like "the soft-float ABI is not required to be stable, as it is an implementation detail of the (Linux?) kernel, which is allowed to have an unstable ABI that changes from version to version"? If so, that is not something very important to us here. That describes the situation for Rust too.

For the Rust compiler's purposes, all that matters is that an ABI can be used by anything for an external call. Sometimes Linux code calls across kernel object boundaries, and sometimes Rust code calls across object boundaries, too. They both manage, despite the need to recompile everything per-version. Stability between compiler or kernel versions is not a required component for us to care about an ABI.

body:
> -mbackchain
> -mno-backchain
>    Store (do not store) the address of the caller's frame as backchain pointer into the callee's stack frame. A backchain may be needed to allow debugging using tools that do not understand DWARF 2 call frame information. When -mno-packed-stack is in effect, the backchain pointer is stored at the bottom of the stack frame; when -mpacked-stack is in effect, the backchain is placed into the topmost word of the 96/160 byte register save area.

I was looking at the gcc documentation for `-mbackchain` and... we don't even support emitting DWARF 1, lol? Is this combination actually relevant? Can anything that doesn't support this actually debug Rust, in the kernel or otherwise?

author: workingjubilee

body:
@workingjubilee yes that was what i tried to say. 

I dont know about the other ABI flags. The current behavior of llvm for s390x is, if you enable the `+soft-float` feature it defacto "changes" the ABI to an inofficial soft-float abi. It means that currently there is no `soft-float-abi` parameter that llvm will evaluate. If you set the `+soft-float` feature you enable the soft-float feature and at the same time change the ABI.

because in rust it is undesirable to change the ABI by just enable a feature, I gated the `+soft-float` feature with an explicit check on the `.abi` field. 

Yes, as far as i see, setting the `.abi` field has no technical effects on the compilation. However it seems to be a straight forward way to gate the usage of `soft-float` feature.

body:
I don't think it makes sense to exhaustively list how this is implemented on all platforms. That doesn't say much unless you are an expert in low-level OS APIs.

Instead, under "Platform-specific behavior", couldn't we just say something like:

> If the executable is removed while it is running, the returned path might be invalid.

I think that fits in well with the current documentation structure and tone.

*[View changes since the review](https://triagebot.infra.rust-lang.org/gh-changes-since/rust-lang/rust/150824/32fe406b5e71afbb0d8b95280e50e67d1549224c..93dd428ed4edede2d6a4f66b7eb143e7ac2578a5)*
author: Enselic

body:
> I don't think it makes sense to exhaustively list how this is implemented on all platforms. That doesn't say much unless you are an expert in low-level OS APIs.
> 
> Instead, under "Platform-specific behavior", couldn't we just say something like:
> 
> > If the executable is removed while it is running, the returned path might be invalid.
>
> I think that fits in well with the current documentation structure and tone.

So on the one hand, it is quite of a lot of text to cover all these obscure platforms, and it is in danger of going out of date. And you're quite right that it still doesn't directly tell you what the behavior is, it only gives you a pointer to look it up.

On the other hand, I think people could reasonably want to know how this function handles edge-but-not-niche cases like the file being deleted or renamed while running, or it being hard linked. I came to this from fixing a user-visible bug in Zed, and other threads described how there have been historical security vulnerabilities connected to those edge cases.

My thought was that if we list the underlying OS calls, then people at least have a handle to find out how it specifically behaves on various platforms. Without it, they have to dig through the source code to find out what it calls, which is of course possible, I just did it myself... but that logic taken to an extreme means no API docs are needed at all. 

I thought it was reasonable to add this because of precedents like <https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/fs/struct.Permissions.html#method.readonly> and <https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/fs/fn.symlink_metadata.html> which tell you in detail what the semantics are on Windows and Unix including which particular underlying APIs that they use, for example:

> This function currently corresponds to the lstat function on Unix and the GetFileInformationByHandle function on Windows.

I'm open to rolling this back to just say 

> On Linux, the string `" (deleted)"` is added if the file has been deleted. Note that this may change in future.

I don't see a lot of point in just saying the result "might be invalid", as that seems to leave the user with nothing to do but read the source.

Thanks for reading and replying!

body:
That implementation is via `DefaultABIInfo::EmitVAArg`

https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/blob/c379a88c09af4657d609888a46ada7719b2c9da9/clang/lib/CodeGen/ABIInfoImpl.cpp#L77-L83

To `EmitVAArgInstr`

https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/blob/c379a88c09af4657d609888a46ada7719b2c9da9/clang/lib/CodeGen/ABIInfoImpl.cpp#L404

To the LLVM `CreateVAArg` I believe this will eventually call `expandVAArg`

https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/blob/21a1e6e6a70d70635c68b9aaa54f816ae36a6416/llvm/lib/CodeGen/SelectionDAG/SelectionDAG.cpp#L2515

That really looks like `emitVoidPtrVAArg` to me, but it's hard to be completely sure.
author: folkertdev

body:
Hello target maintainers, just making sure that you all see this. Overall we're just copying what clang does, but let us know if there is anything that doesn't look right or if you have useful background information. Otherwise maybe just thumbs-up the message to indicate that it looks good.

Some context you might be missing: I'm trying to stabilize c-variadic function definitions in rust. Historically, just relying on LLVM has been fragile, so we're replicating what clang does in this file. Doing (some of) our own codegen means we can make stronger promises about the exact behavior.

By making this match exhaustive, we'll know that we've at least considered all of the different targets.

body: The `Write::flush` method should be a noop for `File`s. It's only meant to flush buffered writers to the OS, since files are unbuffered there's nothing to do.
author: the8472

body: I have made it a noop and added a comment for the same. However, while it is a noop on unix and windows, other platforms like motor, solid, etc seem to call normal file flush on it.
author: Ayush1325

body:
I don't know whether those platforms do anything special or it just wasn't caught during review.

CC @lasiotus @kawadakk in case the platforms need fixing.
author: the8472

body:
Thanks for the ping!

Re: flushing a file on Motor OS: the story is a bit complicated: while at the moment the call is a noop, it won't be in the future, as Motor OS is moving towards a fully asynchronous I/O (at the moment file I/O is synchronous) with in-process async runtime, so flushing a file will not stay a noop. The good news is that the call is already plumbed through to Motor OS runtime, so no changes to Rust std are expected.

body:
> We tried that and things broke (IIRC),

On this specfic point, as far as I could test the patch in https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/pull/149622 breaks existing `aarch64-unknown-none` code that uses the `aarch64-rt` and/or the `semihosting` crate. This can be observed in [this example](https://github.com/ferrous-systems/rust-training/tree/742338475e5455fb9123873186fa47a6f1f7fe05/example-code/qemu-aarch64v8a): QEMU hangs and no output is printed; but the behavior can be observed in simpler programs as well.

As the AArch64 v8-R ISA builds on top of the Armv8.4-A ISA taking that patch has the same effect on this target.

body:
> Hh... hmm. Would it be better to move this comment to a Rust comment inside the example code block so that it's directly visible in-context and likely to be copied with it?

@workingjubilee

If we move the note into a Rust comment inside the first example, wouldn't it seem local to that specific snippet? Because here we have three snippets that demonstrate the same semantic fact ('ﬃ' to "FFI"), just through different APIs (iteration vs println! vs assert). Putting the explanation in only one of them wouldn't make the other appear to lack the key context?, and I feel like duplicating the same comment in all of them would introduce redundancy. (maybe i'm overthinking it, and you have a great point about being likely to be copied with it)

I think keeping it as an introduction note makes it clear that it applies to the entire Examples section. What do you think?

I don't like how special `Target::Param` is here.

There are other targets, such as `Target::WherePredicate`, that similarly were previously checked separately, and now have to be added to all attributes with `AllowListWarnRest`. We should have some way to ensure that these weird targets are not forgotten, but this doesn't feel right. 

If we do this, we should at least rename `AllowListWarnRest` so the name is something like `AllowListWarnMost`, but even then this feels iffy to me.

body:
Operations like `Duration::ZERO.mul_f32(-5.0)` and `duration.mul_f32(-0.0)` currently return a zero duration without panicking, which should be preserved or need a stronger argument to be changed.

Something like `Duration::from_nanos(1).mul_f32(-1e-37)` is more questionable: It also returns a zero duration, but only because the exact result is a tiny negative value that rounds up to `-0.0` (as `f32` seconds). Panicking seems reasonable for those cases (nonzero duration multiplied with a nonzero negative float).
author: quaternic

body: Also, the case with `rhs.is_nan()` should get the panic message for that even if it happens to have a negative sign.
author: quaternic

body: This would further regress precision when scaling down a long duration with a small factor. Consider `rhs == 0.9 / NANOS_PER_SEC`; the match would return `0.9`, and when cast `as u128` that becomes zero.
author: quaternic

body:
